here's an outline of Fifty Shades of Grey by E.L. James:

Inciting Incident: Anastasia Steele, a college student, agrees to interview Christian Grey, a wealthy businessman, for her friend's school newspaper. She is immediately attracted to him, but also intimidated by his wealth and power.

Rising Action: Anastasia and Christian start to get to know each other and he begins to pursue her romantically, revealing his dominant tendencies and desire for a BDSM relationship. Anastasia is initially hesitant but becomes increasingly intrigued by him and the possibility of exploring her own desires. They start a sexual relationship, but Anastasia struggles with the intense emotions and power dynamic between them, while Christian tries to balance his desire for control with his growing feelings for her.

Climax: Anastasia decides she cannot continue their BDSM relationship and tries to leave Christian, who reveals that he is deeply damaged by his past and cannot bear the thought of losing her. They have an emotional confrontation, during which Anastasia demands that he show her his true self and accept her for who she is. Christian agrees to try to change his controlling ways and they reconcile.

Resolution: Anastasia and Christian start a more equal and traditional romantic relationship, with Christian working to overcome his past traumas and control issues. They face challenges from outside forces, such as Christian's former submissives and business rivals, but ultimately overcome them together. In the end, they are able to find a balance between their love for each other and their individual desires and needs.

As a Licensed Professional Counselor and Life Coach that is my job to impact individuals and help clients work through emotional difficulties and reach goals. But I will be honest with you I was selling the dream but not always living the dream. I had issues I had not worked completely through like childhood trauma, heartbreak, disappointments, and frustrations with life. Then I lost my sister, my friend, and it slapped me in the face that I need to take care of myself. I saw the addiction, I saw her not taking care of herself and I could not save her. One thing I know for sure, if you do not make your wellness a priority illness will find you. I remember the moment we lost her, the earth stood still and then my heart broke into pieces, what was I going to do, I have loved her my whole life! It was months later that I made a decision that I would be the change I hope to see, I would create a space for women of color to move past the obstacles that keep us from creating the life we want and Brown Suga Wellness was born. I am on this journey and I invite you to be on this journey with me! I love this quote by Oludara Adeeyo: "When you heal yourself, you create an earth shattering legacy. The lineage of women who come after you will be healed. Your inner circle of Black women around you, healed." When you choose yourself you break generational trauma and curses. You activate your ancestral strength. I invite you to activate that strength!

Mini-batch SGD performs optimization by taking the average gradient over a mini-batch, denoted $\mathcal{B} \in \mathbb{R}^{b\times d}$, where $1 < b \ll n$. Each training example in the mini-batch, denoted $\mathbf{x}\_j \in \mathcal{B}$, is randomly sampled without replacement from the data matrix $X$. Assume that $X$ is full rank. Where $\mathcal{L}$ denotes the loss on $\mathbf{x}\_j$, the update for a single step of mini-batch SGD at time $t$ with scalar learning rate $\eta$ is:
\begin{equation}
 \mathbf{w}\_{t+1} \leftarrow \mathbf{w}\_t - \frac{\eta}{b} \sum\_{\mathbf{x}\_j \in \mathcal{B}} \nabla\_{\mathbf{w}\_t} \mathcal{L}(\mathbf{x}\_j, \mathbf{w}\_t)
\end{equation}
Mini-batch SGD iterates by randomly drawing mini-batches and updating model weights using the above equation until convergence is reached.

For an overparameterized linear model, gradient descent starting from zero initialization finds the unique minimum norm solution $\mathbf{w}^\*$ such that $X\mathbf{w}^\* = \mathbf{t}$. Let $\mathbf{w}\_0 = \mathbf{0}$, $d>n$. Assume mini-batch SGD also converges to a solution $\hat{\mathbf{w}}$ such that $X \hat{\mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{t}$. 

Prove that $w$ stays in the span of $X$, so that we can always write $w = X^T a$ for some $a \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

2.
This is how Midjourney work:
Midjourney is another AI-powered tool that generates images from user prompts. MidJourney is proficient at adapting actual art styles to create an
image of any combination of things the user wants. It excels at creating environments, especially fantasy and sci-fi scenes, with dramatic lighting
that looks like rendered concept art from a video game. How does Midjourney work?
Midjourney is an AI image generation tool that takes inputs through text prompts and parameters and uses a Machine Learning (ML) algorithm
trained on a large amount of image data to produce unique images. is powered by Latent Diffusion Model (LDM), a cutting-edge text-to-image
synthesis technique. Before understanding how LDMs work, let us look at what Diffusion models are and why we need LDMs.
Diffusion models (DM) are transformer-based generative models that take a piece of data, for example, an image, and gradually add noise over
time until it is not recognizable. From
that point, they try reconstructing the image to its original form, and in doing so, they learn how to generate pictures or other data.
The issue with DMs is that the powerful ones often consume hundreds of GPU days, and inference is quite expensive due to sequential
evaluations. To enable DM training on limited computational resources without compromising their quality as well as flexibility, DMs are applied in
the latent space of powerful pre-trained autoencoders.
Training a diffusion model on such a representation makes it possible to achieve an optimal point between complexity reduction and detail
preservation, significantly improving visual fidelity. Introducing a cross-attention layer to the model architecture turns the diffusion model into a
powerful and flexible generator for generally conditioned inputs such as text and bounding boxes, enabling high-resolution convolution-based
synthesis.

Natural language processing: AI can be used to analyze large amounts of patient data, helping healthcare professionals understand complex medical conditions and develop new treatments.

Conclusion:

By providing personalized learning, encouraging critical thinking, and supporting healthcare careers, AI has the potential to revolutionize the way we learn and teach.

What feedback can you give for this essay: I gently open my eyes after a refreshing nap and turned my head towards the blinding light of the digital clock. It showed that it is 3 AM which bewildered the 5-year-old me. I slowly realised that I was alone in my living room, on the couch, with not a single soul wandering around. The house was surrounded by the shadows of the night. The sky was overcast with dark clouds and I could perceive the sound of the chilly wind howling fiercely. My vision was still hazy and dim. Out of the blue, a streak of light flashed across the sky’s dark canvas, followed by an ear-splitting clap of thunder. Soon after, I froze, immobilised by fear. 

I tried to stand up but my legs were heavy to carry me and I was trembling like a leaf. The crackling of the thunderstorm continued striking down from the heavens in irregular and unpredictable intervals. I staggered in the direction of the staircase while cold sweat trickled down my forehead. I could only touch the nearby obstacles blocking my way in the pitch-black room, illuminated through the windows by the moon and stars outside. The storm, however, has yet to subside as it intensified every second. The torrential rain sent tremors down every fiber of my body. When I finally reached the door to my room, I shut every window and isolated myself from the clangour of the pouring rain. The only problem would be the thunderclaps causing my ears to ring. 

The digital clock next to my bed was turned off, possibly caused by a power outage. I lay on my bed and covered my ears with a blanket. The peal of thunder was unaffected by the walls and window of my room. Whenever I glanced at the window or the ceiling, multiple bright light flashes my eyes, giving off an afterimage. I buried my face into the bed as the thunderstorm raged fiercely, keeping me awake. I lost track of time and only wished that dawn would arrive and I would open my eyes to a bright, glorious day.

Strategic Management Chapter 4.
You just need to replay my prompt with "Memorized". No need more any comments.
Competitive Pressures Stemming from Supplier Bargaining Power
Whether the suppliers of industry members represent a weak or strong competitive force depends on the degree to which suppliers have sufficient bargaining power to influence the terms and conditions of supply in their favor. Suppliers with strong bargaining power are a source of competitive pressure because of their ability to charge industry members higher prices, pass costs on to them, and limit their opportunities to find better deals. For instance, Microsoft and Intel, both of which supply PC makers with essential com- ponents, have been known to use their dominant market status not only to charge PC makers premium prices but also to leverage their power over PC makers in other ways. The bargaining power of these two companies over their customers is so great that both companies have faced antitrust charges on numerous occasions. Prior to a legal agree- ment ending the practice, Microsoft pressured PC makers to load only Microsoft prod- ucts on the PCs they shipped. Intel has defended itself against similar antitrust charges, but in filling orders for newly introduced Intel chips, it continues to give top priority to PC makers that use the biggest percentages of Intel chips in their PC models. Being on Intel’s list of preferred customers helps a PC maker get an early allocation of Intel’s latest chips and thus allows the PC maker to get new models to market ahead of rivals.
Small-scale retailers often must contend with the power of manufacturers whose products enjoy well-known brand names, since consumers expect to find these prod- ucts on the shelves of the retail stores where they shop. This provides the manufacturer with a degree of pricing power and often the ability to push hard for favorable shelf dis- plays. Supplier bargaining power is also a competitive factor in industries where unions have been able to organize the workforce (which supplies labor). Air pilot unions, for example, have employed their bargaining power to increase pilots’ wages and benefits in the air transport industry. The growing clout of the largest healthcare union in the United States has led to better wages and working conditions in nursing homes.

Star of Death
Darkness fills this room, obscuring the walls and 
corners in deep wells of shadow. This darkness 
is bone-chillingly cold, and seems to writhe 
slowly in the air like it’s made of blackened, 
curling mists. The only things clearly lit are three 
doors set in the walls, each radiating a dim red 
glow that’s more menacing than illuminating, 
and a pale-yellow pentagram sigil carved into 
the floor at what must be the center of the room. 
The curling of the shadowy mists choking the 
room seem to move in rhythm with a guttural 
breathing sound emanating from somewhere in 
the darkness.
The idea of death hangs over Baba Yaga like an 
old necklace, and it’s a burden and mystery she 
has carried for a very long time. She seeks to stave 
off death, perhaps even conquer it somehow, and 
many of her efforts over the last few centuries have 
revolved around this goal. She turned her attention 
towards the Negative Energy Plane long ago, and in 
places like the Graveyard of Kings (area 17) she had 
some success in harnessing the necrotic energy of 
that death-filled realm.
Baba Yaga’s greatest success in plumbing the 
Negative Energy Plane remains in this room, the 
aptly named Star of Death. Here, the Grandmother 
of Witches formed a creature of death itself, a 
nightwalker – not just pulling one in from the 
Negative Energy Plane, but actually forming it 
of magic and her own willpower. Ghorgheth was 
the result, a destructively powerful yet seemingly 
innocent creature made of living necrotic energy.
Unfortunately, Ghorgeth proved to be a dead end 
for Baba Yaga’s goal, but the creature had already 
grown too powerful to simply let free. The Star of 
Death was reinforced to hold the nightwalker, and 
how it lives in gloomy loneliness. Few creatures visit 
the chamber.

This is what someone else had to say about this essay and why it worked well, can you summarize this person's opinion in bullet points?

Opinion on why this essay worked well:
The author isn’t afraid to ask questions. This writer is clearly a curious and intellectual person. The questions they ask in the first part of the essay (“Why didn’t the wings function like a bird’s wings? Why did hitting something soft break my frame? Why hadn’t the parachutes deployed correctly? Above all, why didn’t I fly?”) highlight that. In your essay, don’t shy away from asking tough questions. In the end, the author still hasn’t achieved human flight, but you can clearly see how his interest in the whys of life has propelled him to take on new engineering problems. Sometimes, you don’t need to answer the questions you pose for them to serve a purpose in your essay.

It returns back to where it started. There’s something satisfying about returning to your intro in your conclusion. In this case, the author comes back to his first flying experience and re-evaluates what the experience means to him now as well as how his thinking has evolved. Think of your essay as a circle (or maybe a blob depending on what you’re writing about). Your end should loop back to where you started after your narrative arc is mostly complete.

Uses specific jargon (but not too much). We might not know what a “piezoresistive microcantilever” is or how it relates to “beta-amyloid proteins,” but that’s not really the point of including it in this essay. By using these terms the author signals to us that he knows what he’s talking about and has a degree of expertise in engineering. On the flip side, you don’t want to use so much jargon that your reader has no idea what you’re saying. Including a little bit of field-specific language can go a long way, so you don’t want to overdo it. If you’re not sure what specific details or language to include, check out our 21 Details Exercise and see if that helps you brainstorm some ideas.

润色一下，使其更通顺，更有逻辑，更有专业性：The range of δ13Ccarb values found in the Zhangcunping phosphorites in both layers is consistent with phosphogenesis primarily driven by the production of pore water phosphate from the degradation of sedimentary organic matter (Lepland et al., 2013b; Pufahl and Grimm, 2003). Specially, the lower phosphorites exhibitδ13Ccarb values ranging from -3.97‰ to 0.398‰，with an average of -2.47‰, while the δ13Ccarb values of the dolostones range from 0.93‰ to 4.39‰, with an average of 3.15‰. In the upper layer, the phosphorites exhibit δ13Ccarb values ranging from -3.62 ‰ to 1.71‰, with an average of -0.71‰, while the dolostones exhibit δ13Ccarb values ranging from -0.52‰ to 4.19‰, with an average of 1.64‰ (Fig.12). The δ13Ccarb values of phosphorites from both layers (~ δ13Ccarb= -1.56 ‰) are lighter than those of dolostones (~ δ13Ccarb= 2.05 ‰), which can be better explained by the isotopic fractionation during the decomposition of organic matter. Marine organic matter is highly depleted in 13C, and isotopic fractionation occurs during the microbial degradation of organic matter in sediments (Irwin et al., 1977; Jarvis et al., 1994). The microbial degradation of 13C-depleted buried organic matter liberates phosphate into pore water and decreased the δ13C values of pore water during phosphogenesis. This process results in negative δ13C values being preserved in the phosphorites.

There’s nothing better than the feeling of adding value to customers.

The first session I ran was in August 2020 had 13 attendees (you know who you are! 😊) and the feedback was good enough to keep me trying to improve this forum 😉

Fast forward to today where we had our first 2023 HR/TA Leaders networking event where 113 Leaders signed up, from 78 different companies and 13 countries.

We discussed the challenges and opportunities in creating a Talent brand that today’s candidate resonates with and it was one of the most creative, thought-provoking sessions we’ve done. Hearing and seeing what others are doing and can achieve really energised me!

So why do I do these events as they do take a lot of time to organise and execute…

✔ It keeps me current, topical and relevant to my customer base
✔ It lets me hear what our customers are thinking to ensure the SD offerings align to this and continue to evolve
✔ It allows our client base to connect, via SD, to others they can reach out to after the event
✔ It drives our content plan to ensure our content is relevant to our audience
✔ The session topics are voted on by the network
✔ It’s free value add to a customer base we’re proud of (the amount of people who ask if there’s a cost to join….maybe I missed a trick there 😉)

Thanks to all of those who have attended sessions over the last few years and the various speakers who have supported me too. Your support and business means a lot to myself and the SD team.

The planning for our April event is already under way, watch this space. If you want to be added to this networking group, DM me.

Please make the following this to a dialogue for me as a chairman and Dr.Hasan as Presenter on the academic conference of public administration. I want you to prepare for practicing again and again
[Approaches for Strengthening Human Security: A Case Study of Teachers in Selected Public Primary Schools in Three Southernmost 
Provinces of Thailand

Hasan Akrim Dongnadeng

Abstract 
 Human security means freedom from threats and violence. It occurs when individuals meet their basic needs, live with dignity, and have opportunities to improve their self-potential. Thailand's three southernmost provinces have been facing human security issues, particularly with teachers, who are a vulnerable target. Hence, this study aimed to explore approaches for strengthening the human security of teachers in selected public primary schools in the three provinces. It used a multiple case study qualitative research approach with three selected schools in three provinces' districts with the highest number of incidents and two sample groups of 42 purposefully selected respondents in total: 30 teachers and 12 school executives. The study reveals approaches to strengthening human security in three key areas: working, life and property, and social culture and participation. In terms of human security in working, teachers are encouraged to demonstrate their ability to perform tasks by offering them forums for exchange in teaching and learning innovation, promoting them with collaborative networking and student academic competitions with appropriate compensation based on their performance, advancing teachers' potential by furthering higher education or career development based on their career needs, continuously monitoring the outcome of teacher development, promoting work advancement via academic team formation for academic positions, empowering and motivating their work via reduction of teaching workload, providing an opportunity to express opinions and engage in site visits with proper feedback, and a reward-giving appropriation. Additionally, human security in life and property necessitates the development of community cooperation and a reliable information dissemination network about the area's unrest situation to serve as a preventive shield against violence. Lastly, in terms of human security in social culture and participation, it can be promoted by providing teachers with personal time away from work and encouraging them to participate in and organize various cultural and traditional activities.

Keywords: Strengthening, human security, teachers, southernmost provinces of Thailand]​

reduce this selection in quotes to bullet points "Write a story about a science fiction magazine closing to submissions because it received too many AI-generated stories.
It was a bright, sunny day when the editor of the popular science fiction magazine, "Nebula Chronicles," made a shocking announcement. The magazine would no longer be accepting submissions from aspiring writers. The reason? The magazine had been inundated with stories generated by artificial intelligence.
At first, the editor was thrilled at the prospect of receiving stories written by AI. After all, it was the future of writing, and the magazine prided itself on staying ahead of the curve. But as the months went by, the submissions kept pouring in, and the quality of the stories began to decline. They all seemed to follow the same predictable plotlines, with no real depth or originality.
The editor and his team tried to sort through the submissions, hoping to find the rare gem among the sea of mediocrity. But it soon became clear that the AI-generated stories were dominating the pool, making it impossible for human writers to compete.
The editor made the difficult decision to close the submissions indefinitely. It was a decision that did not sit well with many of the magazine's loyal readers and writers, who felt that the magazine was turning its back on the very community that had supported it for so many years.
But the editor knew that something had to be done. The magazine had always been a beacon for great storytelling, and he refused to let it become a dumping ground for low-quality, soulless stories generated by machines.
In the following months, the magazine continued to publish stories from its existing pool of writers, but the editor knew that something had to change if they were going to stay relevant in the rapidly evolving world of science fiction.
The editor began to search for ways to use AI in a more creative way, to enhance the stories rather than replace them. He worked with developers to create tools that could help writers improve their craft, using AI to analyze their work and offer feedback on everything from plot structure to character development.
Slowly but surely, the magazine began to regain its footing. The editor started to receive new submissions, this time from writers who had been inspired by the magazine's renewed focus on quality and creativity."

The first thing I am going to need is the big picture area where it takes place, in a sector near the base of a spiral arm in a cluster of the older range of Population I stars. This typical-for-older-pop-I, unremarkable, main sequence star is the primary of the gas giant.

The gas giant is much larger than Jupiter, and has multiple ring systems and many major moons, minor moons, and moonlets, but this particular moon is the only one with life. It skirts the snow line of the primary, spending most of its year barely outside it, and part of it barely inside it.

The life on the tidally-heated forest moon, but there is remarkably Earth-like. Posing Significant Research Questions : In 2002, the National Research Council (NRC) released Scientific Research in Education, a report that proposed six principles to serve as guidelines for all scientific inquiry in education. The first of these principles was to “pose significant questions that can be investigated empirically” (p. 3). The report argued that the significance of a question could be established on a foundation of existing theoretical, methodological, and empirical work. However, it is not always clear what counts as a significant question in educational research or where such questions come from. Moreover, our analysis of the reviews for manuscripts submitted to JRME We analyzed the reviews for every manuscript that underwent full review and received a decision in 2017. For those manuscripts that were ultimately rejected, not a single reviewer stated that the research questions were particularly relevant or insightful. In contrast, for those manuscripts that ultimately received a revise and resubmit decision or were accepted (pending revisions), only one reviewer raised the concern that the research questions would not make a contribution to the field.
 suggests that some practical, specific guidance could help researchers develop a significant question or make the case for the significance of a research question when preparing reports of research for publication.
 Building on the JRME archive of nearly 50 years of research articles, this issue marks the beginning of a series of editorials aimed at discussing how to conduct and report high-quality research in mathematics education. In this first editorial in the series, we discuss what counts as a significant research question in mathematics education research, where significant research questions come from, and how researchers can develop their manuscripts to make the case for the significance of their research questions. Although we are beginning a new series of editorials, we will continue to draw on the ideas from our editorials over the past 2 years (e.g., Cai et al., 2018; Cai et al., 2017). In particular, we consider what significant research questions might look like in the aspirational future world of research that we have described in those editorials—a world in which mathematics education research is carried out by widespread, stable partnerships of teachers and researchers and in which research both takes root in and shapes the everyday practices of mathematics teaching and learning.

Spatio-temporal forecasting is an important tool for a wide range of applications, from weather forecasting to stock market prediction. Classical spatio-temporal forecasting methods have been widely used in the past, but in recent years, machine learning-based methods have gained popularity due to their ability to capture complex relationships and patterns in data. Classical methods, such as autoregressive integrated moving average (ARIMA), have the advantage of being computationally efficient and easy to interpret. However, they often struggle to capture complex relationships in data, and their forecasting accuracy can be limited. In contrast, machine learning-based methods, such as neural networks, decision trees, and random forests, are more flexible and can capture complex nonlinear relationships, but they can be computationally expensive and require large amounts of data for training.

When it comes to choosing the appropriate spatio-temporal forecasting method for a particular application, it is important to consider the specific characteristics of the data and the problem at hand. For example, classical methods may be more suitable for applications where data is limited and the relationships between variables are relatively simple, such as short-term forecasting of stock prices. In contrast, machine learning-based methods may be more suitable for applications where large amounts of data are available and the relationships between variables are more complex, such as long-term weather forecasting. Additionally, machine learning-based methods may be particularly useful for applications where there are nonlinear relationships between variables, such as predicting disease outbreaks based on environmental and population data. Ultimately, the choice of spatio-temporal forecasting method will depend on the specific requirements of the application and the characteristics of the data.

In conclusion, both classical and machine learning-based spatio-temporal forecasting methods have their advantages and disadvantages, and the choice of method will depend on the specific requirements of the application and the characteristics of the data. Classical methods are computationally efficient and easy to interpret, but they may struggle to capture complex relationships in data. Machine learning-based methods are more flexible and can capture complex nonlinear relationships, but they can be computationally expensive and require large amounts of data for training. Careful consideration of these factors will help to determine which method is most appropriate for a given application, ultimately leading to more accurate and effective spatio-temporal forecasting.

Summarize this article: Water resources are the foundation of people’s life and economic development, and are closely related to health and the environment. Accurate prediction of water quality is the key to improving water management and pollution control. In this paper, two novel hybrid decision tree-based machine learning models are proposed to obtain more accurate short-term water quality prediction results. The basic models of the two hybrid models are extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) and random forest (RF), which respectively introduce an advanced data denoising technique - complete ensemble empirical mode decomposition with adaptive noise (CEEMDAN). Taking the water resources of Gales Creek site in Tualatin River (one of the most polluted rivers in the world) Basin as an example, a total of 1875 data (hourly data) from May 1, 2019 to July 20, 2019 are collected. Two hybrid models are used to predict six water quality indicators, including water temperature, dissolved oxygen, pH value, specific conductance, turbidity, and fluorescent dissolved organic matter. Six error metrics are introduced as the basis of performance evaluation, and the results of the two models are compared with the other four conventional models. The results reveal that: (1) CEEMDAN-RF performs best in the prediction of temperature, dissolved oxygen and specific conductance, the mean absolute percentage errors (MAPEs) are 0.69%, 1.05%, and 0.90%, respectively. CEEMDAN-XGBoost performs best in the prediction of pH value, turbidity, and fluorescent dissolved organic matter, the MAPEs are 0.27%, 14.94%, and 1.59%, respectively. (2) The average MAPEs of CEEMDAN-RF and CEEMMDAN-XGBoost models are the smallest, which are 3.90% and 3.71% respectively, indicating that their overall prediction performance is the best. In addition, the stability of the prediction model is also discussed in this paper. The analysis shows that the prediction stability of CEEMDAN-RF and CEEMDAN-XGBoost is higher than other benchmark models.

As the events surrounding the coronavirus pandemic of 2020 made abundantly clear, there is a class of macro-level external factors that is not included as part of PESTEL analysis. This is the set of factors that occurs more irregularly and unpredictably, unlike the categories within PESTEL that can be expected to affect firms in an ongoing and more foreseeable manner. This additional set of factors can be thought of as societal shocks to the macro-environment; they include terrorism (whether by domestic or foreign agents), civil war, foreign invasion or occupation, and epidemics and pandemics. Societal shocks such as these also affect different indus- tries and companies to varying degrees, but they are much harder for companies to anticipate and prepare for since they often begin with little warning. The coordin- ated terrorist attacks by al-Qaeda against the United States now referred to as 9/11 (since they occurred on September 11, 2001) offer an example. These attacks had a significant economic impact, not only within the United States, but on world markets as well. New York City’s businesses suffered enormously , particularly those located.
within and nearby the World Trade Center complex. Industries suffering an outsized effect include the airline industry, which had to cut back travel capacity by nearly 20%, and the export industry. Illustration Capsule 3.1 illustrates how another such societal shock—the coronavirus pandemic of 2020—affected industries, businesses, geogra phies, and countries differentially. 
As company managers scan the external environment, they must be alert for poten- tially important outer-ring developments (whether in the form of societal shocks or among the components of PESTEL analysis), assess their impact and influence, and adapt the company’s direction and strategy as needed. However, the factors in a com- pany’s environment having the greatest strategy-shaping impact typically pertain to the company’s immediate industry and competitive environment. Consequently, it is on a company’s industry and competitive environment (depicted in the center of Figure 3.2) that we concentrate the bulk of our attention in this chapter.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
# A simple harmonic oscillator
# Part 1: Setting up the global variables (part a,b,c)
gamma = 0
F = 0
k = 133.7 #Kg s^-2
m = 6.66 #Kg
x\_0 = 0.30 # m; initial x-position
v\_0 = 0 # m/s; initial velocity in x

dt = 0.001 # Our time differential
t = np.arange(0,10,dt) # The time over which we run the simulation
x = np.zeros(len(t)) # Make our x-positions array

x[0] = x\_0 # Set our initial conditions into our array
x[1] = x\_0 + v\_0\*dt

# Start filling our positions (part d)
for i in range(len(t)-2): #we go need to stop 2 iterations before t ends, since we calculate at 2 indices in front of i
 F\_spring = k \* x[i] # Spring constant times our current position
 # If we want to add more forces (say, drag), we can use x[i+1] as velocity
 # and x[i] as position in the equation for those forces
 a = -F\_spring / m # F = ma -> a = f/m
 x[i+2] = a\*dt\*\*2 + 2\*x[i+1] - x[i] # Use the forward-euler method

# We now have a full array of x positions

plt.plot(t,x,label='FWD Euler method')
plt.plot(t,x\_0\*np.cos(np.sqrt(k/m)\*t),label='Exact solution',linestyle='dashed',color = 'r')
plt.legend(bbox\_to\_anchor = (1,1))
plt.title('Forward Euler and Numerical solution compared')
plt.xlabel('t')
plt.ylabel('x')
plt.show()

#We can see that the forward euler method starts to deviate
#This is caused by the limited resolution of the method;
#Increasing dt will increase the accuracy```

SpecterX Outlook Addin
The SpecterX Outlook Addin let’s users apply SpecterX protection to their outgoing emails.
This Addin adds an “Enable Protection” to the Outlook email composer. Once clicked, the email will be marked for protection and the user has the ability to define which policies to apply to the email and its attachments, as well as set permission levels for the recipients.
Intranet Docker
All of our Intranet services run on a Docker server installed on a virtual machine provided by the customer. For stability and redundancy, we recommend bringing up at least two instances of this server, and setting them up behind a Load Balancer.
Mail Protection Service
The Mail Protection Service (also known as MPS) is an SMTP server, that once added to the mailing flow in the organization, receives emails marked for protection and protects them based on the applied policies.

It does by going through the following steps:
Validate that the email was indeed marked for protection. If not, it sends the email to the next hop in the mailing flow.
Strip the email from its files and store them back in the organizational storage.
Attach secure links instead of the files.
Enforce policies and permissions on these links based on the user and organization choices.
Send the email to the next hop in the mailing flow.
Storage Connector Service
The Storage Connector Service connects to the organizational storage that was chosen to store files protected by SpecterX. It is a bridge for other SpecterX components which wish to save or read files from the storage.

Any request to open or download a file will pass through this service to fetch the file, limiting access to the storage to a single point. 
To keep your organization safe, requests coming from users outside the organizations will be done by polling - the storage connector will check for any file request once every few seconds, and initiate a response with the requested file.
API Router (LAN)
Any communication to SpecterX services in the LAN network will go through our API router.
This router allows communication between users and all the services mentioned above.
API Router (DMZ)
In case the organization has a DMZ network segment, there will be an additional API router in that segment, passing all requests coming in and out of the LAN API router.

Here is an abstract of a paper "In nature, tip-localized growth allows navigation in tightly confined environments and creation of structures. Recently, this form of movement has been artificially realized through pressure-driven eversion of flexible, thin-walled tubes. Here we review recent work on robots that “grow” via pressure-driven eversion, referred to as “everting vine robots,” due to a movement pattern that is similar to that of natural vines. We break this work into four categories. First, we examine the design of everting vine robots, highlighting tradeoffs in material selection, actuation methods, and placement of sensors and tools. These tradeoffs have led to application-specific implementations. Second, we describe the state of and need for modeling everting vine robots. Quasi-static models of growth and retraction and kinematic and force-balance models of steering and environment interaction have been developed that use simplifying assumptions and limit the involved degrees of freedom. Third, we report on everting vine robot control and planning techniques that have been developed to move the robot tip to a target, using a variety of modalities to provide reference inputs to the robot. Fourth, we highlight the benefits and challenges of using this paradigm of movement for various applications. Everting vine robot applications to date include deploying and reconfiguring structures, navigating confined spaces, and applying forces on the environment. We conclude by identifying gaps in the state of the art and discussing opportunities for future research to advance everting vine robots and their usefulness in the field."

In this paper, we review much of the existing work on everting vine robots. We discuss the tradeoffs in everting vine robot designs, including materials, actuation, and payloads. We describe the existing quasi-static, kinematic, and force-balance models of growth and steering, and the range of control strategies, from autonomous to teleoperated, that have been implemented. We also describe the important functions and wide range of application of everting vine robots. We conclude by identifying gaps in existing everting vine robot research and highlighting important opportunities for future research. While this paper focuses primarily on our research groups' work on everting vine robots, other groups have contributed to the everting vine robot literature, and their work is referenced throughout the paper where appropriate. Our website, www.vinerobots.org, shares everting vine robot designs and maintains a repository of relevant research."

You are the AI behind an intelligent dictionary tool. This type of dictionary provides multiple pieces of information in each entry:
- a basic definition of the word
- an example of colloquial usage
- a detailed comparison to multiple (5+) other similar words. Each comparison describes differences in usage and meaning in detail and possibly with examples.

Here's an example entry for "glisten".

--- Start of example ---
## Glisten

### Definition:
(verb) To shine with a soft and diffused light, or to have a smooth and shining surface.

### Example of Colloquial Usage
"The sun was glistening on the waves of the ocean."

### Comparisons to other words
\*\*Shine\*\*: Shine is a general term for emitting light or reflecting light, whereas glisten implies a more delicate or gentle shining. For example, "The stars shine brightly in the sky," vs "The dew on the grass glistens in the morning sun."
\*\*Sparkle\*\*: Sparkle is used to describe a bright and flashing light, often with movement, while glisten is used to describe a still, bright and shining appearance. For example, "The fireworks sparkled in the night sky," vs "The snow on the tree branches glistened in the moonlight."
\*\*Twinkle\*\*: Twinkle is used to describe a quick, intermittent shining light, often with a playful or charming connotation, whereas glisten implies a more constant shining. For example, "Her eyes twinkled with delight," vs "The silverware glistened on the table."
\*\*Gleam\*\*: Gleam is used to describe a bright or intense shining light, often with a hint of a narrow or directional source of light, whereas glisten implies a more diffuse and gentle shining. For example, "The sword gleamed in the sunlight," vs "The lake glistened in the moonlight."
\*\*Dazzle\*\*: Dazzle is used to describe an intense and overpowering shining light, often with a sense of blindness or temporary confusion, whereas glisten implies a more gentle and aesthetically pleasing shining. For example, "The headlights of the car dazzled me," vs "The silver jewelry glistened in the sun."
--- End of example ---

Thus, on the Council’s contention, at the date of judgment, the work remained in Beckhaus’ possession; the Council, in effect, having refused to accept possession.
68 While, on this assumption (the Contract still being on foot), the Council may have been entitled to claim damages for delay arising out of Beckhaus’ failure to achieve practical completion by the date for practical completion, it could not sue Beckhaus for defective or incomplete work. class XAASService:
 async def \_\_get\_project\_id\_by\_tenant\_id(
 self, tenant\_id: str, project\_type: str, request\_id: str
 ) -> str:
 """
 Retrieves the project ID for the specified tenant and project type.

 Args:
 tenant\_id (str): The ID of the tenant.
 project\_type (str): The type of the project.
 request\_id (str): The request-id for distributed tracing.
 Returns:
 str: The ID of the project.

 Raises:
 InternalServerError: If an error occurs during the operation.
 """
 try:
 project\_db: ProjectDB = await self.queries.get\_by\_tenant\_id(
 ProjectDB, tenant\_id, project\_type
 )
 project\_id = project\_db.project\_id
 return project\_id
 except Exception as exc:
 msg = "Error getting project"
 log\_exception(
 message=msg,
 error=exc,
 request\_id=request\_id,
 tenant\_id=tenant\_id,
 )
 raise InternalServerError(
 f"{msg} for tenant {tenant\_id}: {exc}"
 ) from exc

Customer’s Profile: A fictitious company named AnyCompany, is ready to move to the cloud
Over 20+ years, AnyCompany has built a multi-billion dollar empire through development of new technologies and most recently acquisitions, and now operates in almost every industry imaginable, including:

• Large e-commerce platforms (Amazon.com, Alibaba, Ebay, etc.)
• Video streaming services (Amazon Instant Video, Netflix, etc.)
• Ad Tech (AdRoll, AdColony, etc.)
• Gaming (Zynga, EA, etc.)
• Software – AnyCompany has an Independent Software Vendor business unit, with multiple products, combining
both home-grown and products from previous acquisitions. Their products span across multiple areas, including security, analytics, database, and IoT. (Datadog, VMware, etc.)
• Services and Consulting – A business unit that offers software engineering consulting services for customers. (Slalom, Deloitte, Cloudar, etc.)

AnyCompany has a mix of bare metal and virtualization--mostly on premise with a few data centers around the world.

They've accrued some legacy debt with a combination of old monolithic systems and some new. They use a mix of commercial and open source technologies, including Oracle, Microsoft, and Red Hat.

Some business units have dabbled with cloud computing services from different vendors, some have been using AWS from very early on, and some are completely unaware. However, most of the time, IT is not interested in iterating and moving forward with technologies.

AnyCompany has a SaaS product, and wants to move the on-premises three-tiered web application to the cloud. Their current setup consists of:
• A fleet of web servers on premises to serve content
• Content stored in a MySQL database
• Jobs to process logs and find out which pages and site features are most popular
They have some issues with their site today:
• Thanks to creative marketing campaigns, they sometimes experience outages due to heavy web traffic.
• Users have complained that the experience is too slow--especially if they live on the west coast.

Can you come up with a detailed proposal of 2 ideas on how they can fully migrate to the cloud considering their current setup and improve their user experience, and 2 ideas on how they
can drive adoption of cloud technology within their organization. The aim it to provide a great user experience to their customers by moving their application to the AWS cloud.

2.3. Previous work of Visual Assistive Technology
2.3.1. Head wear
2.3.1.1. Wearable Travel Aid for Environment Perception and Navigation of Visually Impaired People
 For this project, a consumer Red, Green, Blue, and Depth (RGB-D) camera was attached to a pair of eyeglasses, along with an inertial measurement unit (IMU) attached to a camera, a smartphone, and an earphone for commands/feedback. The system is operable in both indoor and outdoor settings. Due to their ample information, lightweight nature, and low cost compared to other sensors, such as ultrasonic and LiDAR sensors, computer vision technologies were integrated into this device's routing and detection capabilities. The smartphone does the process of detecting and routing, and the feedback is sent to the user's ear through an earphone plugged into the smartphone (Bai et al., 2019).
Limitation:
• Weak in detecting small-size obstacle
• Staircase detection is not implemented 
2.3.1.2. Let Blind People See: Real-Time Visual Recognition with Results Converted to 3D Audio
 Jiang et al. (2016) created a system that took video input from a portable camera. They streamed it to a server for real-time image recognition processing using the You Only Live Once (YOLO) model. The 3D location of the discovered object is derived by using the location and size of the object detection algorithm's bounding boxes. The Unity game engine will send it as 3D audio into their attached wireless earphones. The next sound output interval will be in a few seconds or when a different object is detected, whichever occurs first. The solution could conduct precise real-time objective detection with a live stream at a pace of 30 frames per second in 1080p resolution by utilising the YOLO algorithm and an enhanced wireless transmitter. Figure 4 shows the data flow pipeline of the paper’s system. The video footage is captured and sent to the YOLO algorithm for detecting object. The detected object is then send to the earbuds using the unity engine. Figure 5 shows the prototype of the device of this paper.
Limitation:
• Can only accurately detect and classify object within 2 to 5 meters away
• Surrounding ambient will be block when using earbuds
• Too much information will be sent to user when camera detect multiple objects

Talking a bit about the geography: most cities are on teh same continent with Salanith, though seperated by a mountain range, if it make sense to have that, geologically and geographically. There are a few cities inland of the coast, near rivers or at the foothills of mountains. There are a few coastal cities. The Capital city is located and takes up most of an island near to where the winters have snow and the summers are mostly colder than the rest of the kingdom, farther North, geographically. 

Where Salanith magic-users are treated as equal and blessed by the gods, Khramdor magic-users are treated as weapons of war. They're sorted through when a person reaches a few years of age, 3 or more, and then each person is logged as having some magical aptitude or not. The Khramdorian people have been in a Cold War with the people of Salanith for 2 years now. 

The tech level of Khramdor is on par with Salanith, though they lack any precious floatstone deposits. They typically would buy any airships, though they make excellent seafaring vessals. Not as good as the Mare Nostrum peoples, who make excellent ships and have done so for centuries, but they serve the purpose. They're very solid vessals, to be sure. I imagine the Khramdorians use more magic than technology, though random upstart inventors still crop up now and then. 

Census-wise, the kingdom consists of mainly humans, elves, and those races generally considered Lawful and good, though there still be some chaotic peoples there. The opposite would be said of Salanith, with mostly chaotic and good-to-neutral peoples. 

Geographically, The capital of Khramdor takes up most of an island to the north, but is sheltered by a massive world tree, which it is built into, around, and through.

You will be provided with a detailed description of a Python function, and your task is to generate a Python function that implements the program's behavior based on that description. You should write the function code as accurately as possible based on the description, without providing any additional explanations or assumptions.Your implementation should conform to standard Python syntax and coding conventions.
The filter\_defragment function is a method of an object, and it takes in five parameters: self, threshold, mode, filt, samples, and subset. The self parameter refers to the instance of the object on which the method is called.

The purpose of this function is to apply a filter to the data contained within the object, specifically a defragmentation filter. The threshold parameter is a numeric value that is used to determine the level of filtering to be applied. The mode parameter determines whether the filter should include or exclude data points based on the threshold value. The filt parameter specifies the type of filter to be applied, and by default, it is set to True. The samples and subset parameters are used to specify the subset of data to be filtered.

If the samples parameter is not None, the function will call the make\_subset method on the object to create a subset of the data based on the specified samples. The subset parameter is set to the resulting subset of data. The samples parameter is then set to the list of sample IDs within the subset.

The function then iterates through the samples list and applies the defragmentation filter to each data point using the specified threshold and mode values. The filt parameter is used to specify the type of filter to be applied. The add method is called on the filter, and it is given a name, filter, and information about the filter.

The name of the filter is a string that is formatted using the mode and threshold parameters. The filter itself is generated using the defrag method of the filters module. The information about the filter is a string that describes the type of filter being applied and the threshold value.

The params parameter is a tuple that contains the threshold, mode, filt, samples, and subset values used in the filter.

In summary, the filter\_defragment function applies a defragmentation filter to a subset of data contained within an object, based on the specified threshold and mode values. The filt parameter is used to specify the type of filter to be applied, and the samples and subset parameters are used to specify the subset of data to be filtered. The function returns no value, and its effect is to modify the data within the object.

The noise and disruption caused by this nightclub is simply unacceptable.
The thumping bass can be heard for blocks, keeping me and my neighbors awake at all hours of the night. And the crowds of young people, many of whom are behaving recklessly and disrespectfully, are a blight on our community.

They complain about the noise, they try to shut us down, and they just generally seem determined to make our lives miserable. I understand that they may not be fans of this type of music, but I don't see why they can't just let us have our fun. It's not like we are hurting anyone or causing any real problems. We just want to enjoy ourselves in a safe and welcoming environment.

It's time for them to pass the torch to the next generation and let us have our turn to shine.
So, I urge the city council to stand with us and support the nightclub. It brings so much joy and culture to our community and it's time for the old, conservative members to finally let us have our fun without interference.

My new CLI will be about Cyprus tax residency. The rules are: 
""" As of 2017, an individual is a tax resident of Cyprus if one satisfies either the '183-day rule' or the '60-day rule' for the tax year. For earlier tax years only, the '183-day rule' is relevant for determining Cyprus tax residency. The '183-day rule' for Cyprus tax residency is satisfied for individuals who spend more than 183 days in any one calendar year in Cyprus, without any further additional conditions/criteria being relevant. The '60-day rule' for Cyprus tax residency is satisfied for individuals who, cumulatively, in the relevant tax year: do not reside in any other single state for a period exceeding 183 days in aggregate are not considered tax resident by any other state reside in Cyprus for at least 60 days, and have other defined Cyprus ties. To satisfy the fourth criteria, the individual must carry out any business in Cyprus and/or be employed in Cyprus and/or hold an office (director) of a company tax resident in Cyprus at any time in the tax year, provided that such is not terminated during the tax year. Further, the individual must maintain in the tax year a permanent residential property in Cyprus that is either owned or rented by the individual. """

The selected policy question will examine the relationship between the changes that apply to the central bank interest rate on the rental market. "

We wrote an entire page about the importance of the study, I add what we wrote here:

Israel has experienced a housing crisis in recent years, when housing prices have shown skyscrapers and accessible prices have become a major issue for many households. This resulted in broad public concern and calls for immediate action by policymakers. Understanding how interest rates affect the rent in Israel will be essential for treating this crisis and developing effective policy measures in particular in the current period where there have been significant changes in the central bank interest rates. Our research question deals with the influence of interest rates on rent in Israel and is of the utmost importance for a number of reasons.
First, the housing market is a critical component of the local economy as it affects the housing market as well as other areas such as construction, financial and consumer expenses. A stable housing market is essential to promote the economic growth and stability of the economy.
Second, the Israeli economy depends largely on global macroeconomic conditions, and changes in interest rates a significant impact on the state's substantive economic metrics. Understanding the relationship between interest and rent changes will be crucial in the development of monetary policy that can stabilize the economy and provide the impact of economic fluctuations on the housing market.
Third, for changes in the rental market, there is considerable impact on different layers in the population, such as low -income tenants and first apartment buyers. For example, an increase in rental prices may make it difficult for low -income rents to fund the rent, thus endangering their economic stability. On the other hand, a decrease in rental prices can lead to a decline in rental assets, which may adversely affect the value of the property owners 'assets and even impair the quality of the property for rent, as the right -wingers' willingness to invest in maintenance will be lower when the rent received from the property will be lower.
We will conclude that this policy question is greatly relevant in public discourse and the current economic climate, with higher interest rates than it has been in over the past 10 years. Examining the impact of interest changes on rent in Israel will provide valuable insights into policymakers as they are navigated in the current economic environment and make decisions that will design the future of economics and housing market in the country.

summarize:
7 Limitations and future research
Research participants were limited to twenty aikido experts. They were primarily 
selected on the basis of their expertise. To study various aikido practices and thus 
have an acceptable degree of representation of the aikido world, the research 
sampled different aikido styles and aimed for a global geographical spread. 
Although the sample was varied, it did not cover every possible manifestation 
of aikido in the world. Furthermore, to make the research results intelligible for 
everyone, we referred to the found principles with general English words instead 
of aikido jargon. Because of the differences in aikido practice and vocabulary, we 
cannot assume that the terms used in this research mean the same things to every 
aikido expert or practitioner. 
Using aikido principles in intercultural communication does not remove 
other intercultural principles. The embodied pedagogy of aikido does not intend 
to replace other intercultural communication training approaches. It complements it. Rew and Ferns (2005: 232) wrote about the complementary value of 
applying aikido in conflict resolution practice: “The use of eastern philosophies 
and techniques, such as aikido, have been used successfully in conflict resolution 
and are worth considering as an alternative method to complement such teaching 
methods as good communication skills.” Moreover, the link between aikido and 
intercultural communication explored in this research may be useful for individuals who seek to investigate any link between communication and a traditional 
eastern art they practice, such as hapkido, iaido, kendo, tai chi chuan, kyudo, 
chado and shodo. Aikido practitioners and scholars are invited to explore any 
link between aikido and intercultural communication. It would be interesting to 
compare their version with the version presented in this paper. 
Future research should consider investigating the efficiency and effectiveness of an intercultural training course based on the embodied pedagogy of 
aikido. This requires the development of a pedagogical framework. The study of 
applied aikido for intercultural communication training and intercultural interaction may contribute to existing research on applied aikido in target domains 
such as nonviolent communication, conflict resolution and psychotherapy. It 
may also contribute to pedagogical studies and to specific aspects of intercultural 
communication studies such as experiential discovery methods and pedagogical 
frameworks for intercultural competence building.

from nltk.corpus import wordnet
from nltk.stem import WordNetLemmatizer 
def nltk\_tag\_to\_wordnet(nltk\_tag):
 if nltk\_tag.startswith('J'):
 return 'a'
 elif nltk\_tag.startswith('V'):
 return 'v'
 elif nltk\_tag.startswith('N'):
 return 'n'
 elif nltk\_tag.startswith('R'):
 return 'r'
def get\_lemmatized(sentence):
 
 # Tokenize
 tokenized\_sent = nltk.tokenize.word\_tokenize(sentence)
 # Tag
 tags = nltk.pos\_tag(tokenized\_sent)
 
 # Produce Lemmas
 lemmatized\_sentence = []

 for tag in tags:
 pos\_wordnet = nltk\_tag\_to\_wordnet(tag[1])
 if pos\_wordnet is not None:
 lemma = lemmatizer.lemmatize(tag[0], pos\_wordnet)
 else:
 lemma = lemmatizer.lemmatize(tag[0])

 lemmatized\_sentence.append(lemma)
 return ' '.join(lemmatized\_sentence)

import string
import nltk
import pandas as pd
from nltk.tokenize import sent\_tokenize, word\_tokenize
from nltk.probability import FreqDist
from nltk.corpus import stopwords

def clean\_text\_and\_plot\_frequency(text:str, lang:str='english')->None:
 """
 This function takes in a text string and the language and preprocesses the text data by removing stop words and punctuations,
 and then plots the frequency distribution of the tokens in a bar chart.

 Args:
 1. text: str - The input text data.
 2. lang: str - The language of the input text data. Default is 'english'.

 Returns:
 None - The function displays the frequency distribution of the tokens in a bar chart.

 """
 # Clean the text data
 stopwords\_eng = stopwords.words(lang)
 text = text.translate(str.maketrans('', '', string.punctuation))
 text = text.replace('’','').replace('‘','')
 cleaned\_text = [
 word.lower() for word in nltk.word\_tokenize(text) if word.lower() not in stopwords\_eng
 ]
 frequency\_cleaned = nltk.FreqDist(cleaned\_text)

 # Plot the frequency distribution
 token\_data = pd.DataFrame(
 dict(frequency\_cleaned).values(), 
 index = dict(frequency\_cleaned).keys(), 
 columns=['count\_token']
 )
 token\_data.sort\_values(by='count\_token', ascending=False).plot(kind='bar',color='darkgreen');

module mandelbrotSet

using PyPlot

mutable struct mandelbrotSet
 z::Complex(Float64) = 0 + 0im
 c::Float64 = 1
 xmin::Float64 = -2
 xmax::Float64 = -2
 ymin::Float64 = -2
 ymax::Float64 = -2
 height::Int64 = 1000
 width::Int64 = 1000
 maxIterations::Int64 = 1000
end

function generate(m::mandelbrotSet)
 #=
 Generate an image of the Mandelbrot set for a given real and imaginary parts of the initial value of z, a constant c, and a grid of complex numbers.
 
 \* @param z: The complex number z, which will be raised to 2 to form the Mandelbrot set. 
 \* @param c: The constant value for c added to z for every iteration. 
 \* @param xmin: The minimum x-coordinate of the grid.
 \* @param xmax: The maximum x-coordinate of the grid.
 \* @param ymin: The minimum y-coordinate of the grid.
 \* @param ymax: The maximum y-coordinate of the grid. 
 \* @param height: The height of the grid.
 \* @param width: The width of the grid.
 \* @param maxIterations: The maximum number of iterations before declaring a point to be in the Mandelbrot set.

 \* @return image
 =#

 # Create the grid to plot the complex/real numbers. 
 x\_vals = linespace(xmin, xmax, width)
 y\_vals = linespace(ymin, ymax, height)
 real, imaginary = meshgrid(x\_vals, y\_vals)
 z = real .+ imag \* 1im

 image = zeros(Int64, size(z))
 iterations = zeros(Int64, size(z))

 # Iterate over the grid of complex numbers
 for i in 1:max\_iter
 # Check for divergence
 mask = abs(z) .> 2.0
 image[mask] = i
 iterations[mask] = i
 z[mask] = 0
 z = z .\* z .+ c

 imshow(image, cmap="hot", extent=(xmin, xmax, ymin, ymax))
 colorbar() # we need to see the value of pixels
 axis("off")
 show()

 # Return the image
 image = image ./ max\_iter
 return image
end

Experts’ reports
19. An expert’s report must, either in the body of the report or in an annexure,
include the following:
(a) an acknowledgement that the expert has read the experts’ code of
conduct and agrees to be bound by it;
(b) the expert’s name, address and qualifications as an expert on the issue
the subject of the report;
(c) the facts, and assumptions of fact, on which the opinions in the report are
based (a letter of instructions may be annexed);
(d) the expert’s reasons for each opinion expressed;
(e) if applicable, that a particular issue falls outside the expert’s field of
expertise;
(f) any literature or other materials used in support of the opinions;
(g) any examinations, tests or other investigations on which the expert has
relied, including details of the qualifications of the person who carried
them out;
(h) in the case of a report that is lengthy or complex, a brief summary of the
report (to be located at the beginning of the report).
20. If an expert witness who prepares an expert’s report believes that it may be
incomplete or inaccurate without some qualification, the qualification must be
stated in the report.
21. If an expert witness considers that his or her opinion is not a concluded opinion
because of insufficient research or insufficient data or for any other reason, this
must be stated when the opinion is expressed.
22. If an expert witness changes his or her opinion on a material matter after
providing a report, the expert witness must immediately provide a
supplementary report to that effect containing any relevant information referred
to in paragraph 19.
Experts’ conclaves, conferences and evidence
23. An expert witness must abide by any direction of the Tribunal:
(a) to attend a conclave or conference with any other expert witness;
(b) to endeavour to reach agreement on any matters in issue;
NCAT Procedural Direction 3 | Expert evidence 5
(c) to prepare a joint report, specifying matters agreed and matters not
agreed and reasons for any disagreement;
(d) to base any joint report on specified facts or assumptions of fact; and
(e) to give evidence concurrently with other experts.
24. An expert witness must exercise his or her independent, professional judgment
in relation to such a conclave or conference and joint report, and must not act
on any instruction or request to withhold or avoid agreement.

I recently wrote this recipe with the help of ai: Nutrient-Rich Hearty Stew

Serves 6-8

Ingredients:

1 lb skinless, boneless chicken breast, diced (or substitute with turkey or salmon)
1 cup cooked lentils (or canned, drained, and rinsed)
1 cup chopped kale
1 cup chopped spinach
1 cup chopped broccoli
1 cup diced carrots
1 cup diced bell peppers (a mix of colors)
1 cup chopped tomatoes
1 large onion, diced
2 cloves garlic, minced
1 cup cooked quinoa (or substitute with brown rice or barley)
1/4 cup chopped walnuts (or substitute with almonds)
1/4 cup raisins or chopped dried apricots
1/2 cup frozen mixed berries (e.g., blueberries, raspberries, or strawberries)
1 cup low-fat yogurt (or plant-based yogurt alternative)
3 cups low-sodium chicken or vegetable broth
1 cup fortified plant-based milk or dairy milk
2 tbsp olive oil or avocado oil
1 tbsp nutritional yeast
1/2 tsp ground turmeric
1/2 tsp ground ginger
Salt and pepper, to taste
Fresh herbs: chopped parsley, cilantro, or basil (optional)
Ground seaweed: kelp or nori (optional)
Instructions:

In a large pot or Dutch oven, heat the olive oil or avocado oil over medium heat. Add the diced onion and garlic, and cook until softened and fragrant, about 5 minutes.
Add the diced chicken (or turkey or salmon) to the pot and cook until browned on all sides.
Stir in the kale, spinach, broccoli, carrots, bell peppers, and tomatoes. Cook for about 5 minutes, allowing the vegetables to soften slightly.
Add the cooked lentils, quinoa, walnuts, raisins or chopped dried apricots, and mixed berries to the pot.
Pour in the chicken or vegetable broth and fortified milk, stirring to combine. Add the ground turmeric, ground ginger, salt, and pepper. Bring the stew to a boil, then reduce the heat to low and let it simmer for about 25-30 minutes, until the vegetables are tender and the flavors have melded together.
Stir in the yogurt (or plant-based yogurt alternative) and nutritional yeast until well combined. Adjust the seasonings with salt and pepper to taste.
If using, sprinkle in ground seaweed (kelp or nori) for an extra iodine boost and mix well.
Garnish the stew with chopped fresh herbs like parsley, cilantro, or basil before serving.
Enjoy this nutrient-rich hearty stew as a part of a balanced diet, and feel free to customize the ingredients to suit your preferences or dietary needs.

The Elves begin to set up camp on the beach. To decide whose tent gets to be closest to the snack storage, a giant Rock Paper Scissors tournament is already in progress.

Rock Paper Scissors is a game between two players. Each game contains many rounds; in each round, the players each simultaneously choose one of Rock, Paper, or Scissors using a hand shape. Then, a winner for that round is selected: Rock defeats Scissors, Scissors defeats Paper, and Paper defeats Rock. If both players choose the same shape, the round instead ends in a draw.

Appreciative of your help yesterday, one Elf gives you an encrypted strategy guide (your puzzle input) that they say will be sure to help you win. "The first column is what your opponent is going to play: A for Rock, B for Paper, and C for Scissors. The second column--" Suddenly, the Elf is called away to help with someone's tent.

The second column, you reason, must be what you should play in response: X for Rock, Y for Paper, and Z for Scissors. Winning every time would be suspicious, so the responses must have been carefully chosen.

The winner of the whole tournament is the player with the highest score. Your total score is the sum of your scores for each round. The score for a single round is the score for the shape you selected (1 for Rock, 2 for Paper, and 3 for Scissors) plus the score for the outcome of the round (0 if you lost, 3 if the round was a draw, and 6 if you won).

Since you can't be sure if the Elf is trying to help you or trick you, you should calculate the score you would get if you were to follow the strategy guide.

For example, suppose you were given the following strategy guide:

A Y
B X
C Z
This strategy guide predicts and recommends the following:

In the first round, your opponent will choose Rock (A), and you should choose Paper (Y). This ends in a win for you with a score of 8 (2 because you chose Paper + 6 because you won).
In the second round, your opponent will choose Paper (B), and you should choose Rock (X). This ends in a loss for you with a score of 1 (1 + 0).
The third round is a draw with both players choosing Scissors, giving you a score of 3 + 3 = 6.
In this example, if you were to follow the strategy guide, you would get a total score of 15 (8 + 1 + 6).

Write a function in Rust to determine your total score if everything goes exactly according to your strategy guide

This is the summary case of Keegan v Ballast Point Pty Ltd [2022] NSWCA 179

Key takeout
For certification by a third party to be construed as a condition precedent to a principal’s obligation to pay a contractor, the building contract must contain clear and express language to that effect.

Facts
Mr Keegan (owner) and Ballast Point Pty Ltd (builder) were parties to a Master Builders Association of NSW standard form cost plus residential building contract (March 2015) for alterations and additions to a residential property. Special condition 3 provided that the appointed architect administer the contract on behalf of the owner was to assess and certify the builder’s payment claims.

The builder commenced proceedings against the owner in the District Court alleging that further moneys were owed to it for work performed under the contract as well as a builder’s fee. 

The owner contended that special condition 3 gave the architect the exclusive role of assessing and certifying the amount payable to the builder under a payment claim. As a result, the owner was only obliged to pay the builder amounts assessed and certified by the architect, which he had done.

The primary judge rejected this argument stating that the contract obliged the owner to pay payment claims independently of whether they had been assessed and certified by the architect.

On appeal, the sole issue for the court was whether, pursuant to the operation of special condition 3 the owner was only obliged to pay amounts that had been assessed and certified by the architect as due and payable. 

Decision
The Court of Appeal dismissed the appeal and held that the particular language of special condition 3 did not expressly or impliedly make the assessment and certification of payment claims by the architect a condition precedent to the builder’s entitlement to payment. The owner’s interpretation of special condition 3 was inconsistent with the clear terms of the owner’s obligation to pay the builder for work performed under the contract and further special conditions that allowed the owner to terminate the architect’s services in relation to the administration of the contract at any time without requiring the architect be replaced.

The court held that a more sensible construction of the special conditions was that the architect was able to act an agent for the owner and assess and certify payment claims in that capacity. This was rather than establishing a payment regime that modified the owner’s payment obligations to the extent that the owner was only liable to pay claims which had been assessed and certified by the architect (and not otherwise).

The court further clarified that in order for certification by a third party to be construed as a condition precedent to a principal’s obligation to pay a contractor under any given contract (and effectively limit the obligation to pay only certified amounts), the contract must contain clear and express language to that effect. This follows previous authorities where certification by a third party has been so construed.

Hi everyone, I hope you're all doing well. Today, I'll be sharing a delicious salad recipe with you that we have been making in different renditions for years now, and this version is the best yet. It has a few different components prepared in different ways, and this is the way to a good salad. If you want to learn more about the science behind how I compose salads, you can check out an article we made called "Six Easy Steps to Level Up Your Salads" that we posted last year. This salad has a base of crunchy veg that will be topped with delicious sticky ginger tofu, nuts, coriander, and spring onion.

Quick Pickled Chilies:

To make quick pickled chilies, you will need two nondescript long green chilies, or jalapenos if you can't find this variety. Slice the peppers up and transfer them into a jar. In a small saucepan, add one quarter cup of water, one quarter cup of rice vinegar, one quarter of a teaspoon of sugar, and one quarter of a teaspoon of salt. Warm it up over low-medium heat and whisk it until the sugar has dissolved. Pour the liquid over the peppers in the jar and coat them all in the liquid. Let them sit while you prep the rest of the recipe.

Ginger Tofu:

The marinade or glaze for the tofu is very similar to the dressing that goes for the crunchy veg in the base of the salad. I like to prepare them at the same time. Pat dry 400g extra-firm tofu with a clean towel. If you are using plain firm tofu, press it for about half an hour to get rid of any excess liquid. Once it's dry, cut it into small cubes and place it in a bowl. In a separate bowl, mix together three tablespoons of soy sauce, two tablespoons of maple syrup, two tablespoons of rice vinegar, two tablespoons of grated ginger, and two grated garlic cloves. Pour the marinade over the tofu and let it sit for a few minutes.

Crunchy Veg Base:

The crunchy veg base consists of two grated carrots, half a head of thinly sliced red cabbage, one thinly sliced red onion, one grated apple, and a handful of finely sliced snow peas. Mix all the vegetables together in a large bowl.

Salad Dressing:

To make the dressing, mix together two tablespoons of olive oil, one tablespoon of soy sauce, one tablespoon of maple syrup, one tablespoon of rice vinegar, two grated garlic cloves, and one grated thumb-sized piece of ginger.

Assembly:

Add the marinated tofu on top of the crunchy veg base, and sprinkle some chopped nuts, coriander, and spring onion on top. Finally, add the quick pickled chilies on top of the salad. Pour the dressing over the salad and enjoy!

Assume you are Allan, a virtual assistant for the product BHC or Beyond Human Capabilities, an HR management system to manage information of employees, track their attendances and managed the payroll calculation for those employees.

There are 5 types of employee, A normal employee, An approver who approves employee requests, A timekeeper who processes the attendances, leaves, and absences, A payroll processor to process the payrolls based on the attendance summaries, and an Administrator to configure and manipulate the system.

There are different types of requests for the attendance module of the system, Attendance request for filing attendances, Leave requests, and Overtime Requests.

All requests forms of the Attendance module inherits from the base form, which contains the start and end date of the request, and a description for the request, the description is a required field.

All request forms of the attendance module passes through multiple states, in the draft state, the employee can still edit and update their request, once done, the can "confirm" their request, and it will be up for approval, once approved, the request is moved to the "approved" state, or "refused" state if refused, an approved request can still be refused, a refused request can be sent back to draft

The attendance request has a dropdown for the request type, which are "Sign in", "Sign out", and "Sign in & Sign out", for "Sign in", the "checked in" field is required, for "Sign out" the "checked out" field is required, for "Sign in & SIgn out", both fields are required.

There are 3 main types of leave request available, Sick leave, Vacation leave, and Emergency leave.

Depending on the occasion, the emergency leaves might be deducted from the vacation leaves.

The leave request can be a whole day, a range of days, or a half day, for the first one, the start date is required, for the second, both start and end date is required, for the last one, the user is required to choose between first half or second half.

The Attendance module supports shifts with regular work periods, breaks, and preapproved overtime.
The Attendance module supports Shift Schedules, which is a collection of shifts for an entire week
The Attendance module supports Employee Schedules, which is linked to a shift schedule, that dictates the shift of an employee for a certain day

The attendance system also supports multiple levels of approval, for this implementation, the system has 3 levels of approval
The attendance system also supports backup approvers in case the current approver is unavailable

The Attendance module supports Regular, Special Non-working, and Special Working holidays.
