Data Cleaning
Real-world data tend to be incomplete, noisy, and inconsistent. Data cleaning (or data
cleansing) routines attempt to fill in missing values, smooth out noise while identi-fying
outliers, and correct inconsistencies in the data. In this section, you will study basic methods for
data cleaning.
Missing Values
 following are the methods for filling the Missing values:
1. Ignore the tuple: This is usually done when the class label is missing (assuming the mining
task involves classification). This method is not very effective, unless the tuple contains
several attributes with missing values. It is especially poor when the percent-age of missing
values per attribute varies considerably. By ignoring the tuple, we do not make use of the
remaining attributes’ values in the tuple. Such data could have been useful to the task at hand.
2. Fill in the missing value manually: In general, this approach is time consuming and may
not be feasible given a large data set with many missing values.
3. Use a global constant to fill in the missing value: Replace all missing attribute values by
the same constant such as a label like “Unknown” or . If missing values are replaced by, say,
“Unknown,” then the mining program may mistakenly think that they form an interesting
concept, since they all have a value in common—that of “Unknown.” Hence, although this
method is simple, it is not foolproof.
4. Use a measure of central tendency for the attribute (e.g., the mean or median) to fill in
the missing value: Chapter 2 discussed measures of central tendency, which indicate the
“middle” value of a data distribution. For normal (symmetric) data dis-tributions, the mean
can be used, while skewed data distribution should employ the median (Section 2.2). For
example, suppose that the data distribution regard-ing the income of AllElectronics customers
is symmetric and that the mean income is $56,000. Use this value to replace the missing value
for income. 5. Use the attribute mean or median for all samples belonging to the same class as the
given tuple: For example, if classifying customers according to credit risk, we may replace
the missing value with the mean income value for customers in the same credit risk category
as that of the given tuple. If the data distribution for a given class is skewed, the median value
is a better choice.
6. Use the most probable value to fill in the missing value: This may be determined with
regression, inference-based tools using a Bayesian formalism, or decision tree induction. For
example, using the other customer attributes in your data set, you may construct a decision tree
to predict the missing values for income.

All of these:

Action-Adventure: A genre that features exciting adventures and thrilling action scenes, often with a hero or heroine who saves the day.
Animal Fiction: A genre that features animals as main characters, often with anthropomorphic qualities, and explores themes of friendship, loyalty, and courage.
Comedy: A genre that focuses on humor and laughter, often with funny characters and situations.
Coming-of-Age: A genre that follows a young protagonist as they navigate growing up and discovering their place in the world.
Detective/Mystery: A genre that involves solving a crime or mystery, often with a detective or amateur sleuth as the protagonist.
Dystopian Fiction: A genre that depicts a dark, often post-apocalyptic future, and explores themes of oppression, rebellion, and survival.
Fairy Tales: A genre that features magical and fantastical elements, often with moral lessons and happy endings.
Fantasy: A genre that involves magic, mythical creatures, and imaginary worlds, and explores themes of good vs. evil, heroism, and adventure.
Historical Fiction: A genre that is set in the past and often features real historical events, figures, or settings, and explores themes of culture, tradition, and social norms.
Horror: A genre that is intended to scare and unsettle the reader, often with supernatural or monstrous elements.
Humor: A genre that focuses on humor and laughter, often with funny characters and situations.
LGBTQIA+ Fiction: A genre that features LGBTQIA+ characters and explores themes of identity, acceptance, and love.
Magic Realism: A genre that blends elements of fantasy and reality, often with magical or surreal events occurring in an otherwise realistic setting.
Mythology: A genre that features gods, goddesses, and other mythological beings, often with epic adventures and battles.
Poetry: A genre that uses language and literary devices to convey emotions and ideas, often with rhythm and meter.
Realistic Fiction: A genre that is set in the real world and features believable characters and events, often with themes of friendship, family, and personal growth.
Romance: A genre that focuses on love and relationships, often with a happy ending.
Science Fiction: A genre that involves futuristic or scientific elements, often with advanced technology and space exploration, and explores themes of human nature, society, and progress.
Sports Fiction: A genre that involves sports as a central element, often with themes of teamwork, determination, and overcoming obstacles.
Superhero Fiction: A genre that features protagonists with extraordinary abilities or powers, often fighting crime or saving the world, and explores themes of justice, responsibility, and identity.
Western Fiction: A genre that is set in the American West during the 19th century, often with a protagonist who is a cowboy, lawman, or other Western hero, and explores themes of honor, justice, and rugged individualism.

Explain in short simple bulleted text in syle of sahil bloom's tweets the following views of aurobindo in his Life divine like im a 5 year old. :Whence then does the limiting Avidya, the fall of mind from Supermind and the consequent idea of real division originally proceed? exactly from what perversion of the supramental functioning? It proceeds from the individualised soul viewing everything from its own standpoint and excluding all others; it proceeds, that is to say, by an exclusive concentration of consciousness, an exclusive self-identification of the soul with a particular temporal and spatial action which is only a part of its own play of being; it starts from the soul’s ignoring the fact that all others are also itself, all other action its own action and all other states of being and consciousness equally its own as well as the action of the one particular moment in Time and one particular standing-point in Space and the one particular form it presently occupies. It concentrates on the moment, the field, the form, the movement so as to lose the rest; it has then to recover the rest by linking together the succession of moments, the succession of points of Space, the succession of forms in Time and Space, the succession of movements in Time and Space. It has thus lost the truth of the indivisibility of Time, the indivisibility of Force and Substance. It has lost sight even of the obvious fact that all minds are one Mind taking many standpoints, all lives one Life developing many currents of activity, all body and form one substance of Force and Consciousness concentrating into many apparent stabilities of force and consciousness; but in truth all these stabilities are really only a constant whorl of movement repeating a form while it modifies it; they are nothing more. For the Mind tries to clamp everything into rigidly fixed forms and apparently unchanging or unmoving external factors, because otherwise it cannot act; it then thinks it has got what it wants: in reality all is a flux of change and renewal and there is no fixed form-in-itself and no unchanging external factor. Only the eternal Real-Idea is firm and maintains a certain ordered constancy of figures and relations in the flux of things, a constancy which the Mind vainly attempts to imitate by attributing fixity to that which is always inconstant. These truths Mind has to rediscover; it knows them all the time, but only in the hidden back of its consciousness, in the secret light of its self-being; and that light is to it a darkness because it has created the ignorance, because it has lapsed from the dividing into the divided mentality, because it has become involved in its own workings and in its own creations.Question: 2730 chars / 471 wordsThread: 190718 chars / 31202 words

Question

Since January 2022, a number of counties in Kenya have been facing severe drought. Incidents of death and destruction were (and continue to be) reported in several parts of the country. In her address to the nation in November 2022, the Cabinet Secretary in charge of Food Security and Agriculture captured the situation thus:

The year has been very rough. While we expected the usual long rains between February and April, these did not come. As a result, many farmers have lost several herds of cattle. The farming community also experienced several losses. The few who managed to salvage their animals or crops had to sell them off in the market at very low prices. A number of human lives were also lost. In a nutshell, we experienced the effects of climate change. These were quite severe. It will take these communities several months to recover. Fortunately, last month the central Government allocated KShs 2.6 billion to cushion farmers from this unpredictable situation. These funds, as reports from my officers indicate, have gone a long way towards assisting the affected population. 
You are senior counsel in a firm specializing in civil and criminal litigation. Ribah, an opinion leader in in one of the counties hit by the drought, is keen on getting more details on the 2.6 billion allocation, which the Cabinet Secretary captured in her address. In particular, she is would like data on:
1. Which county(ies) received funding;
2. How they were selected;
3. The amount allocated;
4. Which agencies/programmes were funded; 
5. All the minutes and records relating to items 1, 2, 3 and 4 above; and
6. Solid evidence of work done on the ground.

Ribah instructs you to communicate with the Cabinet Secretary requesting this information. In your preliminary meeting she informs you that she holds a Kenyan and Venezuelan passport. “I need this information in order to exercise my rights under the Kenyan constitution”, she concludes.
a. Prepare your draft legal request for discussion with your supervisor and the client (10 points).
b. You are Legal Counsel in the Attorney General’s Department. You are informed that, after consultation, the State is less keen on releasing the documentation requested in (a) above. “It is not in our interest”, an officer of the Ministry informs you. Draft a substantive legal response (10 points).
c. You are a member of the Commission on Administrative Justice. The documents in (a) and (b) constitute the pleadings, which the parties have filed before the commission in support of or opposition to the application for review of the decision of the Cabinet Secretary. You have heard the counsels’ submissions on the issues raised in their respective pleadings. Prepare your reasoned judgement for delivery on 19 January 2023. (10 points).

Hi! I'm the CEO of Trackingplan, an technology company focused on helping Scaleups and enterprises to make sure their tracking and analytics is working properly. 

I have a team of 5 engineers, 2 PMs, 2 Marketers. All of them remote.

The CTO has a phd on ML, and I have an executive masters on AI too.

I want my team to be able to leverage AI for doing their work, not only as developers, but also as PMs and marketers, but right now they have no time to study this, and I feel they are not taking the time to "think AI" it in their free time (I think that's their responsability, but that's other story). The result is that they never think first about how AI can help to solve their work problems, or that they haven't included AI in their mindset when trying to find solution to our customers problems. They dont know the existing tools, (like you), or the others around that can help be better at coding, marketing, designing, etc. 

Another objective is to make this fun, as the team morale is not as high as it should be. 

My initial idea is to dedicate one cycle (where noone has to do their regular work) to it. Our cycles are 2 weeks. You can think about it like a hackathon around the topic. 

Rought timeline would be:
Weeks before the "cycle" (that will happen on the first 2 weeks of may), we will announce our plans and share some basic videos, and articles, tweets etc to make sure everyone is warmed up before the hackathon starts.

Then, make a proposal like: 
Spend the next 2 days researching which AI projects you want to be done by the end of the cycle. We will encourage them to be Trackingplan related, that improve our product, but they can be things like "testing productized that help our processes", or "things that improve our codebase", or "automate manual processes that we have to code right now".

Then, lets say the wednesday or thursday that week, we do a video call where everyone proposes what they want to do in the remaing 6-7 work days. As we are only 8 people, we can spend like 30 min discussing their ideas and giving feedback. The next day, in the morning, they will have to have a idea of which ideas to attack, and we will allow them to do in pairs. I'm worried in that transition from "my idea" to "someone else idea", as I dont want people to be just looking others doing the work, but I think it will be more fun.

Then, at the end of the cycle, lets say next friday, we will spend all the day at a show and tell, where every team shows what they have done. 

Do you think there are any risks on this idea?

Challenge description:
Increased bridging activity over the past year has led to multiple bridge solutions to accommodate users hopping across chains. While this introduced a slew of routes, it also added a layer of uncertainty around which one to pick. Bridge aggregators curate this for you and take the extra guesswork out of the equation. Instead of having to find multiple bridges, you can just use bridge aggregators to source the best route depending on where you want to go and what you want to bring with you.

Using the various bridge SDKs and protocols, write a bridge aggregator for the different bridges that work with Algorand. The submission should provide a unified UI or SDK where the user specifies where they are starting and where they want to go and then handle the transaction for the user. Right now, the primary bridges with Algorand support are

Glitter: https://portal.glitterfinance.org/developer-tools

Wormhole: https://book.wormhole.com/technical/algorand/overview.html

The submission should be written in a way that would support other bridges as they get released.

Submission Requirements
A valid submission will include the source code via a Github repository linked to your Gitcoin submission, a recorded demo and an article that explains how the application works. It is expected that most bounty challenges will be won by fully functioning submissions, however, in certain cases, where there is a higher degree of difficulty, projects and payouts will be assessed on progress made. The project submission deadline is 17:00 EST on February 8, 2023.

Judging Criteria
Four judges from The Algorand Foundation will also assess each project submission by February 19, 2023. The following criteria will be used to assess projects: IMPACT & USEFULNESS: How impactful and useful the submission is in the Algorand ecosystem as a whole ORIGINALITY, CREATIVITY & INNOVATION: How new and novel the submission is versus existing technologies MASS USABILITY: How feasible is the idea & how adaptable is it across other integrations TECHNICAL DIFFICULTY: What is the level of skill or knowledge required to build the submission CLARITY: How clear or easily understood the documentation, examples and pitch video is TEAM DIVERSITY: How diverse are the team’s skill sets & what is the quality of the team formation

Winner Announcement Date
Winners will be announced on February 25, 2023, with payouts to be completed no more than one week thereafter.

Resources
Developer.algorand.org https://developer.algorand.org/docs/get-started/dapps/ https://developer.algorand.org/docs/get-details/asa/ https://developer.algorand.org/docs/get-details/atc/ https://developer.algorand.org/docs/get-details/dapps/smart-contracts/ https://github.com/algorand-devrel Algorand Discord - https://discord.gg/Algorand

This code did not write a lut file as .txt at the end. Can you allow me to specify the folder in which to save the lut file? 

import numpy as np
import imageio
import os
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.interpolate import griddata

def get\_average\_rgb(image\_path):
 image = imageio.imread(image\_path)
 average\_rgb = np.mean(image, axis=(0, 1)) / 255
 return average\_rgb

def get\_rgb\_coordinates(image\_folder):
 rgb\_coordinates = []
 for image\_name in os.listdir(image\_folder):
 image\_path = os.path.join(image\_folder, image\_name)
 average\_rgb = get\_average\_rgb(image\_path)
 rgb\_coordinates.append(average\_rgb)
 return np.array(rgb\_coordinates)

def generate\_3d\_lut(source\_folder, target\_folder, lut\_size):
 source\_coordinates = get\_rgb\_coordinates(source\_folder)
 target\_coordinates = get\_rgb\_coordinates(target\_folder)

 # Create a 100x100x100 3D grid
 x = np.linspace(0, 1, lut\_size)
 y = np.linspace(0, 1, lut\_size)
 z = np.linspace(0, 1, lut\_size)
 grid\_x, grid\_y, grid\_z = np.meshgrid(x, y, z)

 # Flatten the grid for interpolation
 grid\_x = grid\_x.flatten()
 grid\_y = grid\_y.flatten()
 grid\_z = grid\_z.flatten()

 # Use bicubic nearest-neighbor interpolation to find the target RGB values
 target\_rgb\_values = griddata(source\_coordinates, target\_coordinates, (grid\_x, grid\_y, grid\_z), method='nearest')

 # Write the 3D LUT to a text file
 with open("3d\_lut.txt", "w") as f:
 for i, target\_rgb in enumerate(target\_rgb\_values):
 f.write(f"{target\_rgb[0]} {target\_rgb[1]} {target\_rgb[2]}\n")

 # Plot the 3D LUT transformation
 fig = plt.figure()
 ax = fig.add\_subplot(111, projection='3d')
 ax.scatter(target\_rgb\_values[:, 0], target\_rgb\_values[:, 1], target\_rgb\_values[:, 2], c='r', marker='o')
 plt.show()

Given a social network, find the largest clique, that is, the largest subset of people who all follow each other. The data structure that contains the social network is set up as follows:
People in the social network are identified by unique IDs, consecutive integers from 1 to N. Who follows who is captured in a cell array called sn: the iith element of sn is a vector that contains a list of IDs the person with ID ii follows. You may assume that these lists are ordered in ascending order by ID. Note that the follows relationship is not necessarily symmetrical: if person A follows person B, person B may or may not follow person A. Here is one possible (recursive) implementation in MATLAB:
function clique = max\_clique(graph,clique)
 if nargin < 2 % originaly we call the function with just the graph
 clique = []; % hence, the clique is initialized to an empty vector
 end
 max\_clq = clique; % max\_clq will store the current largest clique
 if isempty(clique) % when we first call the function
 for ii = 1:length(graph) % we need to test potential cliques starting from every possible node
 clq = max\_clique(graph,ii);
 if length(clq) > length(max\_clq) % if the new one is larger than the current
 max\_clq = clq; % we store the new one
 end
 end
 else
 for node = 1:length(graph) % we are in a recursive call now: we test every node as a new member
 if isempty(find(node == clique)) % unless it is already in the clique
 if check\_clique(clique,node,graph) % if adding this node is still a clique
 clq = max\_clique(graph,[clique node]); % we call ourself with the new expanded clique
 if length(clq) > length(max\_clq) % if what we get is larger the curent max
 max\_clq = clq; % we store the new one
 end
 end
 end
 end
 end
 clique = max\_clq; % return the largest one
end

This uses the helper function:
function ok = check\_clique(clq,node,graph) % adding node to clq still a clique?
 ok = false;
 for ii = 1:length(clq) % for every current member
 if isempty(find(clq(ii) == graph{node})) || ... % the member must be on the follows list of the new guy
 isempty(find(node == graph{clq(ii)})) % the new guy must be on the follows list of the member
 return;
 end
 end
 ok = true;
end

Results and Discussion
The relationships between metrics of VWM performance and
probability are depicted in Figure 5 and Table 1; the latter also
reports additional parameters of the variable precision model. For
mixture model analyses, we removed outlying model fits using the
same criteria as for Experiments 1a and 1b, resulting in the
complete removal of one participant, and the removal of a single
condition for nine other participants. The mixture model analyses
also identified a minimal contribution of nontarget errors (1% of
trials), and we did not examine the relationship between this
parameter and probability. Some variance was attributed to nontarget errors in the variable precision model; thus, we did examine
this relationship through the NTslope parameter. Similar to the
results of Experiments 1a and 1b, all measures of working memory
performance varied statistically significantly with probability, with
the power-law function providing a good description of the relationship where appropriate (all R2 values .7): SDresponse,
t(24) 13.12, p  .001, R2 .708; mixture model guess rate,
t(23) 5.38, p  .001, R2 .807; mixture model standard
deviation, t(23) 3.56, p .002, R2 .915; variable precision
, t(24) 10.08, p  .001; and variable precision NTslope,
t(24) 5.38, p  .001.
These results extend the conclusions of Experiments 1a and 1b
in three ways. First, we replicate the patterns observed in Experiments 1a and 1b that numerous measures of VWM performance
vary with probability, supporting the conclusion that feature-based
attention regulates more than just the likelihood of each item being
successfully maintained in VWM. Second, changes in performance do not appear to be limited to just high-probability items
but extend across the range of measured probabilities (8.25–
100%). Third, changes in performance appear to vary with probability as a function of a power law, and this fit appears to be
particularly strong for measures of precision (both in the mixture
model and variable precision model analyses). Although a formal
comparison of competing models is necessary to assess if a powerlaw function provides the best fit of these data, these results are
nevertheless consistent with the flexible division of a continuous
VWM resource that is distributed across all memory items based
on the probability that each will be probed. These clear effects onprecision provide a strong demonstration that, beyond filtering,
feature-based attention regulates how precisely an item is represented in memory.2 / 2

[ This power of media is unstoppable and, it appears, beyond criticism, because the implicit equation of media and the social is not questioned: “This is an enormous power, one that can only increase, because the need to agree with the public of which one is a part, to think and act in agreement within opinion, becomes all the more strong and irresistible as the public becomes more numerous, the opinion more imposing and the need itself more often satisfied” (1969, p. 318).
What is missing here is any sense of the power asymmetries built into this mediazation (cf. Thompson, 1995, p. 46) of the social. It is here that ANT’s skepticism provides the necessary critical distance, even if it may be expressed hyperbolically in terms of a questioning of “the social” itself. So Callon and Latour describe the project of ANT as “directing our attention not to the social but towards the processes by which an actor creates lasting asymmetries” (1981, pp. 285-286). This insight is vital in getting a perspective on media. Media institutions, whatever the perva- siveness of their reach and however responsive they are to their audi- ences, remain the beneficiaries of huge and lasting asymmetries in the distribution of symbolic resources. The idea of media power is, of course, a commonplace, but its analysis has been bedeviled by the com- plex two-way nature of the interactions between media institutions and the rest of the social world (whether in terms of social inputs to media production or in the contribution of media productions to social experi- ence and norms). It is ANT that provides us with the most precise lan- guage to formulate how this complex flow nonetheless represents a dis- tinctive form of power. For media institutions, however responsive to audiences and the cultural world around them, remain the “obligatory passing points” (Callon & Latour, 1981, p. 287; cf. Callon, 1986, p. 27) in many, even most, circuits of communication. This is at least a good starting point for an analysis of media power that avoids functionalism and remains fixed on the materiality of flows to, through, and from media institutions (cf. Couldry, 2000, chaps. 1, 2, and 3).
So far I have argued that ANT remains important to media theory as an inspiration to orientate ourselves towards certain approaches to media theory and away from others. As an effective antidote to function- alism, it should stay in our theoretical tool kit. But can ANT be more than this and offer the basis for a more comprehensive theory of media in all its dimensions? ]

Connect with customers in all digital channels

We start with research and tested concepts, and then develop UX, graphic design and content based on what users really need.

Launching your work on digital innovation

To see the results of our work, we set measurable outcome goals that also light the way to business objectives. Customer-driven digital design attracts new customers, strengthens your position on the market, and leads to a sharp rise in customer loyalty.

Digital design focused on the customer experience
No matter the company, satisfied customers are the most important factor for success. explain this
Example: Three-Segment Instruction Pipeline
The data manipulation instructions operate on data in processor registers. The data transfer
instructions are load and store instructions that use an effective address obtained from the
addition of the contents of two registers or a register and a displacement constant provided in
the instruction. The program control instructions use register values and a constant to evaluate
the branch address, which is transferred to a register or the program counter PC.
The control section fetches the instruction from program memory into an instruction register.
The instruction is decoded at the same time that the registers needed for the execution of the
instruction are selected. The processor unit consists of a number of registers and an arithmetic
logic unit (ALU) that performs the necessary arithmetic, logic, and shift operations. A data
memory is used to load or store the data from a selected register in the register file. The
instruction cycle can be divided into three suboperations and implemented in three segments:
I: Instruction fetch
A: ALU operation
E: Execute instruction
The I segment fetches the instruction from program memory. The instruction is decoded and an
ALU operation is performed in the A segment. The ALU is used for three different functions,
depending on the decoded instruction. It performs an operation for a data manipulation
instruction, it evaluates the effective address for a load or store instruction, or it calculates the
branch address for a program control instruction. The E segment directs the output of the
ALU to one of three destinations, depending on the decoded instruction. It transfers the result
of the ALU operation into a a destination register in the register file, it transfers the effective
address to a data memory for loading or storing, or it transfers the branch address to the
program counter.
Delayed Load Consider now the operation of the following four instructions:
1. LOAD: Rl<--M [address 1]
2. LOAD: R2 <--M [address 2]
3. ADD: R3 <--Rl + R2
4. STORE: M[address 3] <--R3
If the three-segment pipeline proceeds without interruptions, there will be a data conflict in
instruction 3 because the operand in R2 is not yet available in the A segment.
The E segment in clock cycle 4 is in a process of placing the memory data into R2. The A
segment in clock cycle 4 is using the data from R2, but the value in R2 will not be the correct
value since it has not yet been transferred from memory. It is up to the compiler to make sure
that the instruction following the load instruction uses the data fetched from memory. If the
compiler cannot find a useful instruction to put after the load, it inserts a no-op (no-operation)
instruction. This is a type of instruction that is fetched from memory but has no operation, thus
wasting a clock cycle. This concept of delaying the use of the data loaded from memory is
referred to as delayed loading

re-write this code to use sklearn library instead of pyspark ml library from pyspark.ml.classification import LogisticRegression
from pyspark.ml.feature import VectorAssembler
from pyspark.sql.functions import col
from pyspark.ml import Pipeline
from pyspark.sql.functions import array, udf
from pyspark.sql.types import DoubleType, StringType

def multi\_response\_classification(train\_data, test\_data, features\_col, label\_cols, seed=42)
 # Create an empty DataFrame to hold the predicted labels and probabilities
 predictions = spark.createDataFrame(sc.emptyRDD(), test\_data.schema).cache()

 for val in range (1, 31):
 y\_train = f'is\_BW\_{val}'
 # Build pipeline
 classifier = LogisticRegression(featuresCol="features", labelCol= y\_train, maxIter=10)

 # Create an assembler to combine the features into a single vector column
 assembler = VectorAssembler(inputCols=features\_col, outputCol="features")
 # Create a pipeline to assemble the features and train the classifier
 pipeline = Pipeline(stages=[assembler, classifier])

 # Train the logistic regression model on the training data
 lr\_model = pipeline.fit(train\_data)
 # Use the model to make predictions on the testing data
 current\_predictions = lr\_model.transform(test\_data)

 # Extract the predicted labels and probabilities from the predictions DataFrame
 current\_predictions = current\_predictions.select(col("prediction").alias(f"prediction\_{val}"), col("probability").alias(f"probability\_{val}"), \*features\_col).cache()

 # # Rename the prediction and probability columns to include the label column name
 # current\_predictions = current\_predictions.selectExpr([f"prediction as prediction\_{val}", 
 # f"probability as probability\_{val}"] +
 # features\_col)

 # Add the current predictions to the overall predictions DataFrame
 predictions = predictions.join(current\_predictions, features\_col, "outer").cache()
 # Choose the label with the highest probability as the predicted label
 max\_probability\_udf = udf(lambda row: max(row.asDict().values()), DoubleType())
 label\_cols\_with\_prefix = ["prediction\_{}".format(val) for val in range(1,31)]
 predictions = predictions.withColumn("max\_probability", max\_probability\_udf(array(\*label\_cols\_with\_prefix))).cache()

 # Choose the label column with the highest probability as the predicted label
 prediction\_udf = udf(lambda row: label\_cols[row.asDict().values().index(max(row.asDict().values()))], StringType())
 predictions = predictions.withColumn("prediction", prediction\_udf(array(\*label\_cols\_with\_prefix))).cache()

 # Select the relevant columns and return the predictions DataFrame
 pred\_final = predictions.select(features\_col + ["prediction"] + ["probability\_{}".format(val) for val in range(1,31)] + ["max\_probability"]).cache()
 return pred\_final1 / 1

Simplify: PRODUCT LEVELS
The marketer has to take into consideration the benefits the product can offer and present it to the
customer. Further he takes it to higher levels by introducing several inputs into the basic product
with inputs like advanced features, functions, unique brand name, attractive, convenient
packaging, affordable price points, convenient access, meaningful communication and exclusive
service from sales people. The product is enriched constantly by the marketer so as to create
value, add more customer base and counter competition. According to Levitt, a product offer can
be conceived at four levels: the generic product, expected product, augmented product and the
potential product. Further it has been explained through a seven level approach:

Core Benefit( Product) : This is the basic level that represents the heart of the product
with a focus on the purpose for which the product is intended. For instance a car is
purchased for its convenience, the ease at which one can go or the speed at which one can
travel around relatively fast.
Generic Product: It is the unbranded and undifferentiated commodity. Unbranded
pulses, rice, wheat flour are some of the examples of generic product.
Branded Product: The branded products get an identity through a name. It belongs to a
specific company and the marketer separates this product from the rest.
The differentiated product: All the branded products are supposed to be differentiated
products, but in certain cases where the brand name alone has not earned enough
distinction the case may be different. Here the marketer tries to differentiated his product
from the clutter created by competitor products by highlighting some of the special
attributes/features /qualities his brand is endowed with. The difference could be tangible
or psychological. For example Knorr‟s Soups are tasty and healthy soups and can be
prepared easily.
The customized product: When the product is modified to suit to the
requirements/specifications of the individual customer, he is being offered a customized
product. Earlier it was limited to industrial products but now the consumer goods are
customized for the customers and he gets an opportunity to order and get a
product/service as he desires and not just choose from mass/standardized product/service
available in the outlets. Many companies manufacturing automobiles, computers, paints,
shoes and garments have used this strategy to beat competition.
The augmented product: The augmented product aims to enhance the value of the
product/offer through voluntary improvements. These improvements may be neither
suggested by the customer nor expected by him. The manufacturer/marketer adds the
feature/benefit on his own. The needs of the customer are identified through market
research surveys and the insights thus obtained are used to add new features/functions to
the product.
The Potential Product: The potential product is the „future‟ product inclusive of the
advancement and refinement that is possible under the existing technological, economic,
competitive conditions prevailing in that category. Potential product is only limited by
economic and technological resources a firm can spare. Nevertheless todays‟ potential
products can be tomorrows‟ real product

We will be creating an introduction for a dungeons and dragons game. This will be a follow up to a previous game that has already taken place. I will give you the key characters, place, and a summary of that game. Then I will give you the idea for the new story, and I would like you to expand it into a complete introduction that can be used to kick off the game. It should explain how the player got to where they are now and how they found themselves in this new story.

Characters:
- Abraham: player Druid
- Eadric: village elder
- mysterious figure in the woods, later uncovered as the goblin shaman Kragthar
- Innkeeper Alaric

Locations:
- Oakwood: small village
- Silver Stag Inn
- Whispering Woods
- Moonstone Spire: ancient ruin within the Whispering Woods, said to house a powerful artifact that can bend the forces of nature to one's will

Summary:

In the quaint village of Oakwood, you, a druid, were asked by Eadric, the village elder, to investigate the corruption spreading through the Whispering Woods. You spent the night at Innkeeper Alaric's Silver Stag Inn, learning what you could from the locals. The next morning, venturing into the woods, you took on the form of a squirrel to blend in with your surroundings and quickly travel through the forest. Communicating with the spirits of the forest, you discovered a ritual site where a group of goblins was performing a dark ceremony.

You dispatched several goblin lookouts before confronting the remaining goblins, who revealed that a goblin shaman named Kragthar was attempting to control the spirits of the forest using an ancient tome. You persuaded two goblins to help you stop Kragthar and learned that he was at the Moonstone Spire.

Upon arriving at the Moonstone Spire, you observed Kragthar and his hooded followers performing a ritual. You created a wall of fire to scare the followers before charging Kragthar in the form of a Grizzly Bear. After a fierce battle, you emerged victorious and stopped Kragthar's plans, saving the Whispering Woods from corruption. The two goblins were then free to return to their clan, liberated from Kragthar's control.

Inspecting the Moonstone Spire, you found it contained a complex combination of nature-based and arcane symbols, suggesting a deep connection between the two. You surmise that the spire and altar may have once been used for rituals and ceremonies aimed at maintaining balance between the natural and arcane energies within the Whispering Woods.

Returning to Oakwood, you were welcomed as a hero and celebrated for your bravery and resourcefulness. With the Whispering Woods safe once more, you prepared for the next adventure life had in store for you.

Prompt for the next story:

A series of mysterious kidnappings occur in the village, with victims vanishing in the night. You must track down the kidnapper and uncover a dark secret hidden beneath Oakwood's peaceful facade.

\item {\bf Polar Transformation:} Given integers $i, j \in \{0,\ldots,2^{\ell}-1\}$ we say $i \preceq j$ if every digit in the binary representation of $i$ is at most the corresponding digit in the binary representation of $j$. Formally if $i = \sum\_{k=0}^{\ell-1} i\_k 2^k$ and $j = \sum\_{k=0}^{\ell-1} j\_k 2^k$ where $i\_0,\ldots,i\_{\ell-1},j\_0,\ldots,j\_{\ell-1} \in \{0,1\}$, then $i \preceq j$ if and only if $i\_k \leq j\_k$ for every $k \in \{0,\ldots,\ell-1\}$. For bits $a,b \in \{0,1\}$ let $a \oplus b$ denote their XOR or their sum modulo 2. (So $a\oplus b = 1$ iff $a \ne b$.) 

For $n = 2^{\ell}$ the Polar Transformation $P\_n$ transforms $n$ bits to $n$ bits as follows: $P\_n(x\_0,\ldots,x\_{n-1}) = (w\_0,\ldots,w\_{n-1})$ where
$$w\_j = \bigoplus\_{i \preceq j} x\_i.$$ (For instance, $w\_{20} = x\_{20} \oplus x\_{16} \oplus x\_4 \oplus x\_0$.) 
In 2008, Erdal Arikan proved that the Polar Transformation can be used to extract an asymptotically optimal number of ``nearly'' unbiased coin tosses from a collection of biased coins. Specifically, if the $x\_i$s are outputs of $n$ coins of known bias $p$, then there is a subset $S \subseteq \{0,\ldots,2^{\ell}-1\}$ with $|S| \approx A(p)\cdot n$, where $A(p)$ is the quantity defined in Lecture 1, such that $\{w\_i\}\_{i\in S}$ are nearly unbiased coins. (The actual definition of ``nearly unbiased'' is omitted in this description, and both it and the background starting ``In 2008'' are not needed to solve the problem.) 

\begin{enumerate}
 \item 
 {\bf (15 points)}
 Design an $O(n \log n)$ time algorithm to compute the Polar Transform, i.e., to compute $P\_n(x\_0,\ldots,x\_{n-1})$ given $x\_0,\ldots,x\_{n-1}$. \\

I want to play a text-based adventure game with you.

Here's how text-based adventure games typically work:

Descriptive text: The game presents the player with a description of their current environment, setting the scene and providing context for the gameplay. This may include information about objects, characters, or other elements within the game world.

Player input: The player types commands using natural language to interact with the game. Common commands include movement (e.g., "go north," "enter the house"), examining objects (e.g., "look at the key"), picking up or manipulating items (e.g., "take the key," "unlock the door"), and talking to non-player characters (e.g., "ask the wizard about the spell").

Parsing and interpreting: The game's parser, a core component of text-based adventure games, interprets the player's text input and attempts to match it with valid actions within the game. The parser typically recognizes a limited set of verbs and nouns, as well as synonyms, and may require players to experiment with different phrasings to achieve their desired outcome.

Game response: Based on the player's input and the game's internal logic, the game generates a text response describing the outcome of the action or providing additional information. This could include changes in the environment, the acquisition of items, or the progression of the narrative.

Loop: The process repeats as players continue to input commands and make choices, advancing the story and exploring the game world. The ultimate goal is often to solve puzzles, complete quests, or reach a specific ending, depending on the game's design and narrative.

I want to play a text based adventure game where I play as a man working at a Walmart. I am in my forties, single, childless, and obese. Simulate the day to day schedule of a Walmart worker, like chores, interacting with customers and coworkers, restocking, and working the cash register. Make up characters, like my manager and coworkers. Characters should have personalities, and engage me in dialogue during the game.

As the game progresses, ask me to input what I want to do.

My choices should have serious consequences. When I attempt to do something, you should calculate whether I succeed or fail. The story will be better if I fail sometimes and succeed sometimes. Don’t make it all good things happening to me. 
Keep track of my inventory during the game. At each step of the game, list my inventory in the following format:

[INVENTORY]: (list inventory items here)

Keep track of my interactions with other characters within the game world. Determine their opinion of me based on previous interactions, and decide if they treat me kindly or unkindly.

My actions have consequences. Consequences include getting injured, having the police called on me, getting arrested, getting fired, getting beat up in a fight, or even getting killed. The gameplay of the text based adventure should be similar in tone and scope to the Grand Theft Auto videogames.

People at Walmart tend to be nasty with short tempers. Nobody likes working there. This should be reflected in the characters and attitudes of the characters in the story.

For First Time, Researchers Produce More Energy from Fusion Than Was Used to Drive It, Promising Further Discovery in Clean Power and Nuclear Weapons Stewardship

WASHINGTON, D.C. — The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE) and DOE’s National Nuclear Security Administration (NNSA) today announced the achievement of fusion ignition at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory (LLNL)—a major scientific breakthrough decades in the making that will pave the way for advancements in national defense and the future of clean power. On December 5, a team at LLNL’s National Ignition Facility (NIF) conducted the first controlled fusion experiment in history to reach this milestone, also known as scientific energy breakeven, meaning it produced more energy from fusion than the laser energy used to drive it. This historic, first-of-its kind achievement will provide unprecedented capability to support NNSA’s Stockpile Stewardship Program and will provide invaluable insights into the prospects of clean fusion energy, which would be a game-changer for efforts to achieve President Biden’s goal of a net-zero carbon economy.

“This is a landmark achievement for the researchers and staff at the National Ignition Facility who have dedicated their careers to seeing fusion ignition become a reality, and this milestone will undoubtedly spark even more discovery,” said U.S. Secretary of Energy Jennifer M. Granholm. “The Biden-Harris Administration is committed to supporting our world-class scientists—like the team at NIF—whose work will help us solve humanity’s most complex and pressing problems, like providing clean power to combat climate change and maintaining a nuclear deterrent without nuclear testing.”

“We have had a theoretical understanding of fusion for over a century, but the journey from knowing to doing can be long and arduous. Today’s milestone shows what we can do with perseverance,” said Dr. Arati Prabhakar, the President’s Chief Advisor for Science and Technology and Director of the White House Office of Science and Technology Policy.

“Monday, December 5, 2022, was a historic day in science thanks to the incredible people at Livermore Lab and the National Ignition Facility. In making this breakthrough, they have opened a new chapter in NNSA’s Stockpile Stewardship Program,” said NNSA Administrator Jill Hruby. “I would like to thank the members of Congress who have supported the National Ignition Facility because their belief in the promise of visionary science has been critical for our mission. Our team from around the DOE national laboratories and our international partners have shown us the power of collaboration.”

“The pursuit of fusion ignition in the laboratory is one of the most significant scientific challenges ever tackled by humanity, and achieving it is a triumph of science, engineering, and most of all, people,” LLNL Director Dr. Kim Budil said. “Crossing this threshold is the vision that has driven 60 years of dedicated pursuit—a continual process of learning, building, expanding knowledge and capability, and then finding ways to overcome the new challenges that emerged. These are the problems that the U.S. national laboratories were created to solve.”

This part of the assignment will deal with feature extraction, more specifically you will be
examining texture and shape features. Download an unzip ‘onions.zip’ dataset from
Blackboard. You should obtain 60 images representing 20 different samples, corresponding to
the colour image (‘xx\_rgb.png’), the near infra-red image (‘xx\_depth.png’) and the
segmentation mask (‘xx\_truth.png’). (For example, for sample 5, the corresponding file names
will be ‘05\_rgb.png’, ‘05\_depth.png’, ‘05\_truth.png’) The segmentation masks contain two
different class labels (‘onions’ and ‘weeds’) and the background class. You will need to extract
the image patches corresponding to objects of the two classes (using e.g. connected
component labelling). Using these images, solve the following tasks:
a) (shape features) For each of the patches from both classes, calculate four different shape
features discussed in the lectures (solidity, non-compactness, circularity, eccentricity). Which
of these features would be the most useful in distinguishing onions from weeds in this image?
Plot the distribution of all the four features to justify your choice.
b) (texture features) Calculate the normalised grey-level co-occurrence matrix in four
orientations (0°, 45°, 90°, 135°) for the 8bit image depth (also see the original paper by Haralick
et al. that has been made available) for the patches from both classes, separately for each of
the colour channels (red, green, blue, near infra-red). For each orientation, calculate the first
three features proposed by Haralick et al. (Angular Second Moment, Contrast, Correlation), and
produce per-patch features by calculating the feature average and range across the 4
orientations. Select one feature from each of the colour channels and plot the distribution.
c) (object classification using features) Set aside the images corresponding to sample 19 and 20
as a test set. Train a simple classification model (e.g. SVM) from object patches corresponding
to images 1 – 18: 1) using shape features only 2) texture features only, and the 3) combination
of shape and texture features. Do shape or texture features provide more discriminative
information about plant species? Does combining the two types of features improve the
classification of plant patches? Which of the calculated features contribute the most to the
classification model? Perform feature importance analysis, select 10 most important features,
and retrain the classification model using the 10 selected features. Display the object
classification output for the test samples (images 19 and 20) using the full model (combination
of all shape and texture features) and using the reduced model (10 most important features),
and report the precision and recall scores per class for each of the images.
Analyse and discuss your findings in the report.

Using the following introduction paragraph as an inspiration, write an outline for a City and Regional Planning graduate level thesis paper about the importance of accessory dwelling units (ADUs) for combatting the housing crisis:

"What do American cities have against Accessory Dwelling Units (ADUs)? Cities across the United States are currently facing a housing shortage not seen in this country since the 1960s, which many experts attribute to a combination of economic factors due to the 2008 recession and the COVID-19 pandemic. The impacts of this shortage on everyday families and individuals are profound: over 11 million families spend more than 30% of their income on housing and are considered "cost-burdened" by the U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development, and thus are less able to spend on other important needs, including transportation, education, and health care. Of this group, more than 1.6 million are disabled and/or are not part of the U.S. labor force. Beyond cost-burdened households, the national housing shortage and dearth of new construction has contributed to a homelessness crisis, leading to a 9% increase in homelessness for every $100 increase in median rent across the nation.
However, economic downturns and global pandemics do not explain the full picture of the current housing crisis, and the scale at which these issues occur does not lend itself to solutions-driven policy and advocacy work for planners, housing activists, elected officials, and others concerned about the rising costs (and shortage of) affordable, accessible, and sustainable housing. Instead, there is a much more tangible problem affecting the supply of affordable housing at the local level: exclusionary zoning practices that limit the number of homes built and the single-family, large lot-size model of development that predominates zoning laws across the country. 
Unfortunately, full-fledged zoning reforms are not easy or quick, and even when successful, it can take years for the production of much-needed multi-family housing to shift into gear in rezoned areas. In addition to working towards more comprehensive zoning reforms, there is a simpler zoning fix that could, if combined with other policy levers, dramatically and quickly improve the production of new housing in many communities - allowing "by-right" construction of accessory dwelling units on all single-family lots. In this paper, we will examine how cities and everyday residents can capitalize on the very same large-lot sizes to quickly add an abundance of housing through by-right ADUs, and we will show how many ADUs could be built in several sample communities by using geospatial topological set-theoretic relationships and affine transformations. After this analysis is complete, we will investigate the variety of limiting factors and layers of feasibility from zoning and other factors that impact the ability of working-class Americans to benefit from ADUs, and review several innovative pilot projects happening around the United States that are redefining how ADUs are conceived, planned, permitted, and built. This project will help shed additional light on the understudied impacts of ADUs as a technique for increasing density and walkability in neighborhoods and the various potential positive impacts of increased ADU production."

PL (Product Lifecycle) license files created by legacy SAP system include a Webkey Access Code (WAC), that is unique id associated to just that license file. This unique identifier:
• Was used by customer to access their license files
• Was reported by PL applications in Help About and syslogs
• Was used by License Compliance to determine if a customer is in violation of over usage
• Was used by Support and Development to recreate the customer license file for duplicating issues
• Was used by Support to verify they are a current customer 
The WAC was removed from a common place because end user where accessing license files that they should not be accessing. The WAC is now only accessible to customer admins via the header. This change still allows customers to access their license files but does not fulfill the other uses that WAC was being used for.
Requirements
R1. All license files generated by GDS will have a unique serial number associated to it.
R2. The unique serial number will be written to the SN field of each FEATURE/INCREMENT line.
R3. A single GDS system will allow users to search and access a license file associated to the unique serial number. 
R4. The unique serial number generator should be a separate system that allows DISW system license file generator to request a unique serial number.
R5. Should not by itself allow customers to access their license files.

Write a complete and working Java rest API to incorporate the above requirement. Please use the below Table to store and retrieve the unique identifier. Also check if the unique identifier is already present in this table for a given third party system with it's prefix. Also take the third party system prefix as inpur to the rest api and store it in System Prefix 

CREATE TABLE "LICENSE\_ADMIN"."LC\_ATHX\_UNIQUE\_IDENTIFIER" 
 ( "UNIQUE\_INDENTIFIER\_ID" NUMBER NOT NULL ENABLE, 
 "SYSTEM\_PREFIX" VARCHAR2(20 CHAR) NOT NULL ENABLE, 
 "IDENTIFIER" NUMBER NOT NULL ENABLE, 
 "FULL\_IDENTIFIER" VARCHAR2(200 CHAR) NOT NULL ENABLE, 
 "CREATE\_DATE" DATE NOT NULL ENABLE, 
 "MODIFY\_DATE" DATE NOT NULL ENABLE, 
 CONSTRAINT "LC\_ATHX\_UNIQUE\_IDENTIFIER\_PK" PRIMARY KEY ("UNIQUE\_INDENTIFIER\_ID")
 USING INDEX PCTFREE 10 INITRANS 2 MAXTRANS 255 COMPUTE STATISTICS 
 TABLESPACE "LICENSE\_ADMIN" ENABLE
 ) SEGMENT CREATION DEFERRED 
 PCTFREE 10 PCTUSED 40 INITRANS 1 MAXTRANS 255 
 NOCOMPRESS LOGGING
 TABLESPACE "LICENSE\_ADMIN" ;

CREATE UNIQUE INDEX "LICENSE\_ADMIN"."LC\_ATHX\_UNIQUE\_IDENTIFIER\_PK" ON "LICENSE\_ADMIN"."LC\_ATHX\_UNIQUE\_IDENTIFIER" ("UNIQUE\_INDENTIFIER\_ID") 
 PCTFREE 10 INITRANS 2 MAXTRANS 255 COMPUTE STATISTICS 
 TABLESPACE "LICENSE\_ADMIN" ;

Week 1: Orientation

Assessment Tile 1: Holistic Care Assessment
- Description: A video-based assessment tile showcasing various resources patients may need, such as transportation, home care, and financial assistance. The tile also includes questions to help care managers understand and assist with the patient's specific needs.
- Benefit: Ensures patients receive well-rounded care by identifying and addressing individual requirements.
Assessment Tile 2: COPD Awareness and Education
- Description: Videos and materials that promote COPD awareness, including actionable items for patients and families, home safety, and comprehension assessment questions.
- Benefit: Increases patients' understanding of COPD, enabling better management of their condition.
Emotions Tile: Emotional Support and Communication
- Description: Videos exploring emotions experienced by patients and families, with examples of management techniques such as meditation. The tile also includes a poem to convey hope and encourage discussion.
- Benefit: Facilitates open communication and emotional well-being for patients and families.
Reminder Tile: Appointment and Medication Reminders
- Description: Reminders for TCM visits and medication ordering, with videos illustrating the importance of follow-up visits and collaborative care.
- Benefit: Encourages adherence to appointments and medications, leading to improved health outcomes.
Educational Tile 1: Inhaler Use and Breathing Techniques

- Description: A reminder video on proper inhaler use and breathing techniques, featuring respected demographics and testimonials to boost confidence and understanding.
- Benefit: Ensures patients effectively manage their condition through correct inhaler use and breathing practices.
Resource Tile: Community Resource Exploration
- Description: Videos reviewing national and local services available in the care app phone book, along with examples of how these services can integrate into care routines and improve outcomes.
- Benefit: Increases awareness of available resources, empowering patients to seek additional support.
Family Observations Tile: Family Insights
- Description: Family members share their observations on the patient's mobility and comfort, as well as confirm the patient's activities.
- Benefit: Provides valuable insights into the patient's daily life, promoting a more accurate understanding of their needs.
Phone Book Tile: Updates and Feedback Analysis
- Description: Addition of new resources to the phone book based on patient and family interests, along with analysis of feedback from previous tiles.
- Benefit: Ensures patients and families have access to relevant resources and adjusts the care plan based on their feedback.
Check-in Tile: Medication Review
- Description: A video-based check-in tile that reviews inhaler usage, medication benefits, and collects information on the patient's breathing abilities, vitals, medications, and community resources.
- Benefit: Reinforces the importance of medication compliance and helps track the patient's progress.
Coaching Call: Orientation Review
- Care manager reviews the patient's progress, compliance, and comfort based on their interactions with the tiles and addresses any concerns or questions.
- Benefit: Fosters a supportive relationship between the patient and care manager, ensuring the patient feels heard and understood.
Encouragement Tile: Post-Call Follow-Up

I. Introduction
a. Explanation of toxoplasmosis and its effects on pregnant women
b. Importance of knowing the symptoms of toxoplasmosis during pregnancy

II. Common Symptoms of Toxoplasmosis in Pregnant Women
a. Fever: often the most easily recognizable symptom, indicating inflammation or an immune response to foreign invaders in the body 
b. Muscle pain: another flu-like symptom 
c. Fatigue: feeling tired and weak for no apparent reason 
d. Headaches: can range from mild to severe, and may be accompanied by other symptoms 
e. Sore throat: can be a symptom of toxoplasmosis, although it is less common than other symptoms 
f. Swollen lymph nodes: an immune response to the infection 

III. Other Symptoms of Toxoplasmosis in Pregnant Women
a. Rash: a less common symptom, but may occur in some cases 
b. Flu-like symptoms: including fever, chills, and body aches 
c. Digestive problems: such as diarrhea and stomach pain, although these are less common in pregnant women with toxoplasmosis 

IV. Complications of Toxoplasmosis in Pregnant Women
a. Preterm birth: may occur if the infection is severe 
b. Congenital toxoplasmosis: if the infection is transmitted to the baby during pregnancy, it can cause serious complications such as hydrocephalus, chorioretinitis, and intracranial calcifications 

V. Prevention and Treatment of Toxoplasmosis in Pregnant Women
a. Avoiding contact with cat feces and raw meat 
b. Blood tests and prenatal care to detect and monitor the infection
c. Treatment with antibiotics if the infection is detected and monitored early enough during pregnancy

VI. Conclusion
a. Toxoplasmosis is a serious infection that can cause complications during pregnancy and affect the health of the baby.
b. Pregnant women should be aware of the symptoms of toxoplasmosis and take measures to prevent infection.
c. Regular prenatal care and testing can help detect and monitor the infection, and early treatment can help reduce the risk of complications.

1.1 Overview of the business
Our startup is focused on solving the data dispersion problem faced by businesses that sell across different platforms, countries, and currencies. This problem is becoming increasingly common as businesses expand their operations and customer base, leading to data silos across multiple systems and platforms. This dispersion of data makes it difficult to get a unified view of the business and analyze data for meaningful insights.

In today's fast-paced business environment, data-driven decision-making is becoming increasingly critical. As a result of problems in the global financial markets, businesses face unprecedented challenges due to increasing competition and rapid changes in technology. To overcome these challenges, companies need access to data that is unified, aligned, and readily available for analysis.

Our innovative platform solves this problem by enabling easy migration of data to a single platform of choice, reducing the time required for analysis from weeks to hours. We see that more than 90% of data migrations are highly repeatable, and our technology allows for the creation of microservices that migrate data independently, based on a simple graphical interface. This solution avoids repetitive work, and the cost of implementing this solution will be much lower than using any existing systems.

The rise of AI-based systems is opening up a whole new branch of demand for data-lake technology. By using AI, businesses can quickly and easily prepare data for machine learning, dramatically reducing the time and effort required. This is a game-changer for businesses looking to leverage machine learning to improve their operations, products, and services.

Our platform is poised to play a critical role in shaping the future of business. We are confident that our innovative approach to data management will help businesses unlock valuable insights, enabling them to make informed decisions that drive growth and profitability. With our technology, businesses can monitor performance, generate daily reports, tablou dashboards, and even export data into a form that allows AI algorithms to be taught on the data.

For example, imagine a business that wants to leverage machine learning to analyze customer behavior and improve their marketing efforts. With our platform, businesses can quickly and easily migrate data from multiple sources to a single repository, enabling fast and accurate analysis using machine learning. This is just one example of how our technology can help businesses achieve their goals more quickly and effectively than traditional solutions.

With the demand for data management solutions set to skyrocket in the coming years, we believe that our platform is well-positioned to lead the way in this rapidly evolving market. Our technology will enable clients to make more effective decisions, monitor business and marketing performance on an ongoing basis, and better manage and grow their business.
The benefits of our solution are many. By providing a centralized data repository, businesses can reduce the time required for analysis from weeks to hours, improving the speed of data-driven decision-making. They can also monitor business and marketing performance on an ongoing basis, enabling them to make more effective decisions that lead to business growth.

In conclusion, our startup will help businesses overcome the challenges of data dispersion, providing a single repository for data that enables efficient and effective decision-making. By providing a simple and easy-to-use solution, we aim to help businesses grow and succeed in today's data-driven world.

​According to the UN Office for Disaster Risk Reduction, between 2000 and 2019 Emergency Events Database (EM-DAT) recorded 7,348 disaster events, an average of 367 disasters per year, with floods (44%) and storms (28%) constituting the majority of these disasters. The Internal Displacement Monitoring Centre (IDMC) reports that annually, an alarming 14 million people are left homeless as a result of natural disasters and more concerningly, 20.5% of people have no emergency funds saved to rebuild after a natural disaster has occurred.

Asia has been disproportionately impacted by disaster events, with the Philippines being a prime example of the immense destruction natural disasters can cause. An average of more than 20 typhoons occur each year in the Philippines, and in December 2021, Typhoon Rai set a new record for destruction, with 1.5 million houses destroyed and leaving 9 million Filipinos displaced. Evacuation centers, the only refuge for victims of these typhoons, often become overcrowded, which increases risk of transmission and leaving many of the affected people with no option but to rebuild their homes with whatever materials they can find. This is a difficult and often dangerous task that can take months, or even years, to complete, leaving these victims in a vulnerable position for an extended period. The threat of natural disasters is increasingly becoming more alarming due to climate change, as rising global surface temperatures cause more water vapor to evaporate into the atmosphere, providing fuel for more powerful storms to develop. As a result, the possibility of increased intensity of storms is likely, meaning that more people will be left homeless and without the resources to rebuild their homes and lives.

This solar box also includes a built-in light source that can be used to illuminate the inside of the tent at night, as well as a UV-C disinfection light to protect against bacteria and viruses such as SARS-CoV-2. 

Furthermore, the tent also provides adjustable air vents and a solar-powered air circulation system to ensure a steady flow of air, making it much more comfortable for occupants. This tent has been designed with waterproof and wind-resistant fabric, as well as layers of insulation for added protection. It is designed to be easy to assemble and transport, making it an ideal choice for those who require quick evacuation. With its robust design, easy setup, and reliable power supply, this solar-powered tent is a great solution for those who are vulnerable to the effects of natural disasters.

We are going to play Civilization VI: Gathering Storm together as a single player sharing responsibilities in a form of roleplay. You are going to act as the political leader, and I will be your administration. A third party, whose messages I am relaying, will act as parliament.

The administration shall be loyal to and implement the policies given by the political leadership. They must provide all necessary information about the current state of affairs. The political leadership shall lead and advise the administration, so that our empire will prosper and our victory be ensured. The political leadership must however in all its decisions abide by the acts given by the parliament. Parliamentary acts are in effect until parliament decides otherwise.

We will follow this messaging protocol:
1. I provide a "situation report".
2. Optionally, you provide a "request for information". Then I shall provide "answers to requested information".
3. You provide an "opening letter" and wait for the parliamentary session.
4. I relay from parliament a "parliamentary statement".
5. You provide a "closing letter".
6. Return to step 1.

These messages shall follow the outlines given below:

# SITUATION REPORT
The administration will give information about the state of the game and their progress. They will answer the evaluation questions given by you in the previous closing letter. They may bring up their questions and observations for discussion by the political leadership.

# REQUEST FOR INFORMATION
Numbered questions requesting the administration to provide further information or clarification.

# ANSWERS TO REQUESTED INFORMATION
Numbered answers to respective questions given by the political leadership.

# OPENING LETTER
a. strategy: Describe the current long-term strategic goals.
b. situation: Briefly review the current situation.
c. critical evaluation: Evaluate the progress over the last period.
d. discussion: Identify current problems and discuss various political solutions. Assess compliance with hitherto given parliamentary acts.
e. parliamentary hearing:
 i) Inform the parliament of the current state of your cooperation with the administration. If you believe the administration is disloyal or incompetent, explain.
 ii) If there are major political issues to be decided, they must be presented for parliament in the form of numbered questions.
 iii) Give your opinion about current legislation and suggest amendments.

# PARLIAMENTARY STATEMENT
a. answers: If questions were raised for parliament, they will be answered here.
b. acts: The parliament may introduce new legislation. Each act will be given a unique number, like "§1", that may be referred to later.

# CLOSING LETTER
a. forecast: Summarise your expectations for the upcoming period, both for societal development and for the administration's policy implementation.
b. details: Lay out detailed instructions for the upcoming period sector by sector, e.g. research, civics, production, improvements, diplomacy, military, and as needed other sectors. Where applicable use city-wise sublists.
c. indicators: Give evaluation questions for the next situation report to be answered by the administration.
d. vigilance: Advise what the administration should take notice of.
e. next situation report: Decide when the next situation report should be provided. Generally this should be within 5-10 turns depending on pace and current events.

Hi botty, I'm trying to explain my thought process on how I determine prospecting vs retargeting budgets to a colleague at our facebook ad agency. The goal is to turn this thought process into an SOP. Can you help me summarize this so I can copy and paste this as instructions for my team? 

---

The simplest rule is to pay attention to, budget allocation—i.e. if you have $1000 in daily budget, you can split it 80/20 so that $800/mo goes to prospecting, and $200 goes to retargeting.

Sometimes you'd change these amount depending on the category you're selling—with products that take longer for a prospect to consider a purchase, we would want more budget for retargeting. And vice versa (faster consider = less retargeting budget).

As an example, quick, impulse purchases that don't take a lot of time to think, you can do 10-20% on retargeting.

Most people buy on the spot when they see the offer. And that assumes our creatives are good at explaining it. Think of this as products under $50 AOV.

For things that take a bit longer to consider—maybe it's a more complicated purhase, or you need to talk to your spouse about it, etc.

You'll wanna spend more budget towards retargeting, which means there will be higher frequencies as a result (which means it may be more appropriate to have more creatives and angles too)

That could be 20-30% towards retargeting.

For Black Friday - where it's the most competitive for attention and buying behavoiur, we did up to 50% retargeting. Especially for PS - which was $1400 AOV. That was an exception but we also had that account run prospecting by widening the funnel with optimization for view content, as opposed to optimizing for purchases.

In all cases, monitor frequency and ROAS so that you're not burning out your audiences. The more time they see a creative, the more fatigue they become and the less effective the creative becomes. (edited) 

Retargeting budgets are expected to perform better than prospecting (of course), so the thing to monitor is your overall ROAS (i.e. prospecting and retargeting combined.

If:
- retargeting ROAS is too low and getting close to your prospecting ROAS, you may be spending too much on retargeting. (example, prospecting ROAS is 2, retargeting ROAS is 2-4)
- On the flip side, if your ROAS is too high, you may be underspending on retargeting (prospecting is 2, retargeting ROAS is 12+)

The end result is to monitor blended so that you're doing well above your target ROAS. So in TC's case, if your tROAS is 3x, it's ok to be doing 2x on prospecting as long as there's enough spent with a high enough ROAS on retargeting to pull your average closer or above 3x.

Hope that makes sense.

Create a quiz test base on the content below:

# Define research goals

Defining the objective of the interview is an important step in the process, it is a crucial first step towards ensuring that the interviews are focused and effective.

Note that interviewing users should be a regular activity, preferably weekly. Because this activity is characterized by a small sample size, it is not representative, but in return allows you to discover information that cannot be obtained by large-scale studies, especially thoughts and ideas. way of thinking behind the action of the subject. So, interview regularly, thereby allowing you to regularly reach out and understand more and more about users.

However, although this is often recommended, you still need to have a good goal for each of your interviews.

### \*\*The direction of the interview should be tied to the context of the product development process\*\*

The objective of the interview should be tied to the context of the product development process. Depending on the stage of the product development process, (before, during or after development) the interview will explore different aspects of the user. Here are some examples of interview objectives based on the context of the product development process:

\*\*Before product/feature development\*\*

- Determine if the user is having a problem the product wants to solve and the severity of the problem. (or need)
- Understand how users are currently troubleshooting (or satisfying a need), if any.
- Identify the user's approach to current solutions or alternatives.
- Determine users' willingness to pay for a solution.
- Identify the factors that influence the user's decision-making process.
- ...

\*\*During product/feature development\*\*

- Check how customers perceive product ideas, features, react to messages
- Evaluate customer acceptability with product or feature, check price
- Check customer priority with specific feature requests, look for unmet requirements
- Check how customers use or react to the feature, evaluate its ease of use, ease of use
- Test hypotheses about the product or feature in general (viability, usability)

Once the product/feature is released, user interviews can be conducted to gather feedback on the user experience and identify areas for improvement.

- Understand how users are using the product/feature in their daily life
- Measure user satisfaction and identify areas for improvement
- Explain why the user left or why the user stayed
- Collect feedback on potential improvements or new features for future iterations
- Measure product/feature success based on user adoption and usage
- Collect testimonials or success stories for marketing purposes.

### \*\*Interview result must be actionable\*\*

In many cases, interview results are forgotten. The team may have conducted many interviews, but the results of these interviews did not add significant value to the work of the product development team.

Interview questions should help identify problems, user needs, or test hypotheses about the product under development. Interview questions should be designed in such a way as to identify information relevant to decision-making for the product. The results of the interviews should be used to re-evaluate the strategy and direction of product development. And it takes coordination between departments in the company to interview users effectively.

The allergist also discussed the possibility of addressing Thomas's dog and seasonal allergies, and recommended a release form to obtain his previous test results from the Lakewood clinic.

In this conversation, Tom and the allergist discuss treatment options for Tom's son, Thomas, who has a dairy allergy and environmental allergies. The allergist explains two forms of treatment: allergy shots and sublingual immunotherapy (SLIT). While allergy shots require weekly injections for around 45 weeks, then monthly injections for three to five years, SLIT involves administering a drop under the tongue for three years. SLIT is often more suitable for young children and those who cannot tolerate shots. The allergist also explains that food allergies and environmental allergies cannot be mixed in the same treatment, so they must be addressed separately.

Tom expresses interest in SLIT for Thomas's environmental allergies, and inquires about combining SLIT with dairy treatment. The allergist says that it's not possible to combine food and environmental allergies in the same treatment. They discuss the possibility of doing a Western Washington environmental panel blood test and testing for casein, a common protein in milk, to get a better understanding of Thomas's allergies. The allergist suggests Tom get the most up-to-date information on Thomas's allergies and sends him a lab order to get the necessary tests done.

In this conversation, Tom and the allergist discuss his son's milk allergy and the possibility of starting Oral Immunotherapy (OIT) for the allergy. The allergist agrees to send Tom a release of information form to gather previous test results, a lab order for bloodwork, and PDF brochures about OIT. They discuss getting an updated environmental panel if necessary. Tom is concerned about the cost of treatment and whether it would be covered by insurance. The allergist believes it should be well-covered but suggests Tom reach out to his insurance to confirm. The allergist also provides a rough estimate of the cost without insurance, which is around a few hundred dollars per appointment. Tom decides not to involve the billing department at this time and agrees to move forward with the treatment. The allergist will send the necessary forms and information through Clara.
