ITEM 7. MANAGEMENT’S DISCUSSION AND ANALYSIS OF FINANCIAL CONDITION AND RESULTS OF OPERATIONS

The following discussion and analysis should be read in conjunction with the consolidated financial statements and the related notes included elsewhere in this Annual Report on Form 10-K. For further discussion of our products and services, technology and competitive strengths, refer to Item 1- Business. For discussion related to changes in financial condition and the results of operations for fiscal year 2021-related items, refer to Part II, Item 7. Management’s Discussion and Analysis of Financial Condition and Results of Operations in our Annual Report on Form 10-K for fiscal year 2021, which was filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission on February 7, 2022.

Overview and 2022 Highlights

Our mission is to accelerate the world’s transition to sustainable energy. We design, develop, manufacture, lease and sell high-performance fully electric vehicles, solar energy generation systems and energy storage products. We also offer maintenance, installation, operation, financial and other services related to our products. Additionally, we are increasingly focused on products and services based on artificial intelligence, robotics and automation.

In 2022, we produced 1,369,611 consumer vehicles and delivered 1,313,851 consumer vehicles, despite ongoing supply chain and logistics challenges and factory shutdowns. We are currently focused on increasing vehicle production, capacity and delivery capabilities, improving and developing battery technologies, improving our FSD capabilities, increasing the affordability and efficiency of our vehicles, bringing new products to market and expanding our global infrastructure.

In 2022, we deployed 6.5 GWh of energy storage products and 348 megawatts of solar energy systems. We are currently focused on ramping production of energy storage products, improving our Solar Roof installation capability and efficiency, and increasing market share of retrofit and new build solar energy systems.

In 2022, we recognized total revenues of $81.46 billion, respectively, representing an increase of $27.64 billion, compared to the prior year. We continue to ramp production, build new manufacturing capacity and expand our operations to enable increased deliveries and deployments of our products and further revenue growth.

In 2022, our net income attributable to common stockholders was $12.56 billion, representing a favorable change of $7.04 billion, compared to the prior year. We continue to focus on improving our profitability through production and operational efficiencies.

We ended 2022 with $22.19 billion in cash and cash equivalents and investments, representing an increase of $4.48 billion from the end of 2021. Our cash flows provided by operating activities during 2022 and 2021 were $14.72 billion and $11.50 billion, respectively, representing an increase of $3.23 billion. Capital expenditures amounted to $7.16 billion during 2022, compared to $6.48 billion during 2021. Sustained growth has allowed our business to generally fund itself, and we will continue investing in a number of capital-intensive projects in upcoming periods.

Management Opportunities, Challenges and Uncertainties and 2023 Outlook

I am creating a two step playbook to teach clients about our hot seats and how to attend. This is the original step written by my boss to get people to attend one a week. It should not be changed and this voice will be the one to use. 

Hot Seats are LIVE 30m coaching session. One person is on the Hot Seat. They bring a question or problem they have in their business. And 3-5 peers and GU coaches ask them questions and give advice on how to solve the problem.

You're able to be a fly on the wall to learn, give advice and learn from the experiences of others. 

Join at least 1 Live Hot Seat per week. And pop in on as many as you would like.
Join in on the next Hot Seat
Let your coach know you want to be on the Hot Seat.
Mark this complete once you've attended one Hot Seat this week. 

We want to add a step before the one created by my boss to give more context for a first time attender. I wrote this first draft. I need it edited to match the voice and flow into the one above. 

Please provide an edited version of this below. 

We know that entrepreneurs that surround themselves with others with similar goals are more likely to succeed. Because of that fact, the hot seats were developed to bring energy to you in a way that gets you unstuck and drives action. 

Our goal is to help you get the most out of attending and being the client on the 30-minute hot seat by sharing tips and what to expect.

This is how it is run:
Step #1: The question/problem is presented (5 minutes)
Step #2: Others in the room ask clarifying questions (10 minutes)
Step #3: Advice is given (10 minutes)
Step #4: Takeaways are clarified (5 minutes)
Step #1: State the problem and ask a question
In this step, you will share your problem and brings one question to the group. Meaning, we will only focus on one question at each hot seat.
When on the hot seat:
A) Be prepared to present one problem so the attendees can understand it quickly
B) Keep it short and sweet. Getting feedback from the group will be shortened if you go over your 5 minutes.
C) Be flexible. You will gain clarity as you move through the steps. As a result, your question or its framing may change. That’s okay.

Step #2: Clarifying questions
In this step, the audience will ask clarifying questions. This is important and will often give you massive clarity around your issue. At the end of this step, we will pause and ask you to restate your question with this new clarity.
When on the hot seat:
A) Listen to questions from all different viewpoints. This is a massive benefit of having a diverse group of people in Growth University.
B) Answer honestly. Be prepared to share numbers or details as asked to get the best feedback
C) Assess if the question you came with is still the question you want to ask

Step #3: Advice
In this step, the attendees will give you advice. The many ideas will be exciting. Make sure you listen to all the ideas before making any final decisions.
When on the hot seat:
A) Keep an open mind. Resist saying things like, “I’ve tried that; it didn’t work,” or “that won’t work for me” This closes you off from new opportunities.
B) Listen and stay silent. This is not your turn to talk; you don’t want to miss a great idea because you are filling the time.
C) Thank those for offering suggestions. Each one gets you closer to a solution.

Step #4: Takeaways
In this step, you will state your takeaways. This is a very important step to prevent overwhelm. This often takes the full 5 minutes to ensure these are specific and actionable.
If you are on the hot seat:
A) Be prepared to share your top takeaway.
B) Stay coachable as we help you make it specific and actionable.
C) Share your takeaways with your coach immediately after the session.

Bonus: Have fun!
Remember to have fun and enjoy the experience! Hot seating can be an excellent opportunity for personal and professional growth, and embracing the opportunity to learn and grow is essential.

O aparelhamento político e ideológico de conselhos regionais e do Conselho Federal de Medicina (CFM) tem dificultado o registro de denúncias contra médicos que adotam comportamentos antiéticos.O Café da Manhã encontrou registros de maus-tratos e negligência a pacientes por motivação política, além de relatos de desqualificação da ciência. Médicos ouvidos se queixam da falta de respaldo dos conselhos médicos, que deveriam fiscalizar e regular o setor, e acusam essas instituições de endossar diretrizes que batem de frente com os códigos de ética da categoria. A radicalização de médicos é um tema que ganhou força nos debates sobre bioética no Brasil, principalmente com a pandemia, quando o encontro do jaleco com a política ficou evidente. Entre os casos notórios de comportamentos antiéticos endossados pelo CFM, está a diretriz que permitiu o uso da cloroquina e da hidroxicloroquina no tratamento da Covid-19, apesar de evidências científicas mostrarem a ineficácia dos medicamentos contra a doença. Médicos e bioeticistas ouvidos pela reportagem também apontam um respaldo corporativista a condutas hostis aos pacientes.O CFM afirma que os médicos são orientados a expressar respeito aos pacientes em todas as circunstâncias. E que, em caso de atendimentos eletivos, médico e paciente têm o direito de optar por não estabelecer vínculo. Nesse caso, segundo o Conselho, o profissional deve manter o cuidado do paciente até que ele encontre outro médico que o acompanhe. Pontos como Aparelhamento de conselhos de medicina dificulta denúncia contra a má prática da profissão; Conselho Federal diz defender respeito a todos pacientes.Encontraram registros de maus-tratos e negligência a

import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import re
import nltk
nltk.download('omw-1.4')
nltk.download('wordnet')
from nltk.corpus import stopwords
from nltk.tokenize import word\_tokenize
from nltk.stem import WordNetLemmatizer
from sklearn.feature\_extraction.text import TfidfVectorizer
from sklearn.model\_selection import KFold
from sklearn.utils.class\_weight import compute\_class\_weight
from sklearn.svm import SVC
from sklearn.metrics import f1\_score
from tqdm import tqdm

# Set seed value
np.random.seed(42)

# Load data
train\_df = pd.read\_csv('train.csv')
test\_df = pd.read\_csv('test.csv')

# Compute class weights
class\_weights = compute\_class\_weight(class\_weight='balanced', classes=np.unique(train\_df['label']), y=train\_df['label'])

# Define text pre-processing functions
def preprocess\_text(text):
 # Remove non-alphabetic characters
 text = re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z\s]', '', text)
 # Convert to lowercase
 text = text.lower()
 # Tokenize
 tokens = word\_tokenize(text)
 # Remove stopwords
 tokens = [token for token in tokens if token not in stopwords.words('english')]
 # Lemmatize
 lemmatizer = WordNetLemmatizer()
 tokens = [lemmatizer.lemmatize(token) for token in tokens]
 # Join tokens back into a string
 text = ' '.join(tokens)
 return text

# Preprocess train and test text data with progress bar
train\_df['text'] = tqdm(train\_df['text'].apply(preprocess\_text))
test\_df['text'] = tqdm(test\_df['text'].apply(preprocess\_text))

# Define KFold cross-validation with progress bar
kf = tqdm(KFold(n\_splits=5, shuffle=True, random\_state=42))

# Define TF-IDF vectorizer
tfidf = TfidfVectorizer()

# Initialize F1 scores
f1\_scores = []

# Train and evaluate model for each fold with progress bar
for fold, (train\_index, val\_index) in enumerate(kf.split(train\_df)):

 # Get train and validation data
 X\_train, y\_train = train\_df.iloc[train\_index]['text'], train\_df.iloc[train\_index]['label']
 X\_val, y\_val = train\_df.iloc[val\_index]['text'], train\_df.iloc[val\_index]['label']

 # Compute TF-IDF features
 X\_train\_tfidf = tfidf.fit\_transform(X\_train)
 X\_val\_tfidf = tfidf.transform(X\_val)

 # Train SVM model with class weights
 svm = SVC(class\_weight={i: w for i, w in enumerate(class\_weights)}, kernel='linear', random\_state=42)
 svm.fit(X\_train\_tfidf, y\_train)

 # Evaluate on validation set
 y\_val\_pred = svm.predict(X\_val\_tfidf)
 f1 = f1\_score(y\_val, y\_val\_pred, average='weighted')
 f1\_scores.append(f1)

 # Save predictions on test set for this fold
 X\_test\_tfidf = tfidf.transform(test\_df['text'])
 y\_test\_pred = svm.predict(X\_test\_tfidf)
 test\_df[f'fold{fold+1}'] = y\_test\_pred

 # Update progress bar and print F1 score
 kf.set\_description(f'Fold {fold+1} F1 score: {f1:.4f}')
 kf.update()

# Compute average F1 score
print(f'Average F1 score: {np.mean(f1\_scores):.4f}')

# Ensemble predictions
#Ensemble predictions from all folds
test\_df['label'] = test\_df[[f'fold{fold+1}' for fold in range(5)]].mode(axis=1)[0]

#Save predictions to file
test\_df[['id', 'label']].to\_csv('./0316\_test.csv', index=False)

print("Ensemble predictions completed.")

#Done!

My name is Simon. I'm 21 years old as of now and I have always been obsessed with knowledge. Even as a child, I would spend hours reading books and asking endless questions. I hunger for the secrets of the world, the things that are hidden from us mere mortals. It's what drives me, what keeps me up at night, and what makes me feel alive.

I have lived my entire life within the walls of my family's library. It's where I feel most at home, surrounded by books and the knowledge they hold. But outside those walls, I know there is a dark and dangerous world. A world where monsters roam and death is a constant threat. It's a world that terrifies me, but also intrigues me.

Growing up in the library, I was homeschooled by my parents who were both scholars themselves. They taught me everything they knew, from ancient languages to advanced mathematics(?). But as I grew older, I started to crave more knowledge, knowledge that went beyond what they could teach me.

I would sneak out of the library at night and wander through the streets, listening to the conversations of strangers and reading any books I could find. One night, while sneaking around the city, I stumbled upon a group of scholars who were discussing a mysterious text they had found. Intrigued, I approached them and asked to see the text. They were hesitant at first, but when I showed them my knowledge of ancient languages, they allowed me to join their discussion.

That text changed my life. It contained information about a hidden library, one that held knowledge beyond anything I had ever imagined. I became obsessed with finding this library and thus became my life goals and dream to find it.

As for the celestials, I have always been in awe of their power and beauty. But at the same time, I can't help but wonder what they are hiding from us. What secrets do they keep locked away in their celestial palaces? What is their true nature?

What raises my spirit is the pursuit of knowledge. When I discover something new, something that no one else knows, it fills me with a sense of accomplishment. But what terrorizes me is the fear of the unknown. The fear that there are secrets out there that I may never uncover.

I often feel like I am different from others around me. I see the world through a different lens than most people, one that is focused on knowledge and discovery. It's what drives me, what makes me feel alive. But sometimes, it's also what sets me apart from others. I struggle to connect with people who don't share my passion for learning. It's not that I don't enjoy socializing or spending time with others, but I often find myself feeling like an outsider. It's like we're speaking different languages or living in different worlds. And that can be lonely at times. But at the end of the day, my passion for knowledge and discovery is what defines me. It's what makes me who I am. And I wouldn't trade that for anything.

Summary: The text follows the story of Simon is a geeky, eccentric seeker of knowledge, when he was a kid he was curious about the whispers of a group of wizards. The wizards mention a legend of a hidden library, which becomes simon's life goals to find. Time skip. Simon is now in vendrikan and meets Despho Madden, a legendary figure in Vendrikan society, who destroys an ancient scroll that contained the clue to the hidden library. Despho and an owl lead Simon to a dead end, and the owl speaks on behalf of Despho, saying that Simon is a newborn and will find out about the banquet soon enough. Simon is frustrated that no one will tell him about the banquet and has tried to eavesdrop on conversations but has gotten no information. The entity, Apple, offers Simon the role of a devoted prophet and gives him a spell book and a task to deliver a magical scroll to Del-Shadeau without opening it. Simon enters Snow's Inn, where he meets a young man who offers him a towel and a warm drink. Simon asks about the siblings and their family, revealing that he was an only child. Simon experiences strange occurrences in his room and is interrupted by Despho, who hands him an incomplete map. Simon tries to refocus on his studies but eventually falls asleep.

hello! I would like you're help building a slack app that sends job failure and success notifications. I found a lot of documentation on the topic, can I provide you this information over a series of messages? Here's the first instructions, below. When you are ready for more, respond OK.

Creating Slack Messages from Platform
Avatar Tori Becker
Updated 1 year ago
Part 1: Creating the Slack Credential 
Part 2: Creating the Platform Credential 
Part 3: Running the Platform Job 
Part 1: Creating the Slack Credential 
Click on the workspace dropdown in the top left of Slack, and select “Customize [Workspace name]”:

mceclip0.png

 

This will take you to the web browser of Slack. Click “Menu” in the top left corner, followed by “Configure apps”: 

mceclip1.png
 

That will take you to the Slack App Directory. Then click “Build” in the main toolbar:

mceclip2.png

 

Click “Create New App”. It's common for the app to have to be approved by the Slack administrator at your organization:

mceclip3.png


You’ll now be prompted to follow a series of set up steps. You should follow the Slack documentation to get to the level of access you’ll need for your given use case. Here are some help docs that helped us as we made this: 

https://api.slack.com/authentication/basics#calling
https://api.slack.com/messaging/composing/layouts#attachments
 
 

If you’re planning to use the Platform to Slack script template in part 3, you’ll need the Slack app to at least have the following features and functionalities added (screenshot below), as well as the specific OAuth scopes (Slack’s term for permissions), found under “Permissions”:

mceclip4.png

 

mceclip5.png

 

Note: the OAuth Scopes in Slack have great descriptions, as shown above. Use those to determine if you need to add additional scopes for your use case. You can also use incoming-webhooks to create messages from Google Forms, etc. 


Once the scopes have been added, it’s time to request your app be installed by your administrator. This will allow you to get the OAuth token you’ll need to make a Platform credential. You can do this in the “OAuth & Permissions” page of your app, click “Request to Install”: 

mceclip6.png

Once your admin approves of the app, Slack will alert you by sending a Slackbot message like this: 

mceclip7.png

Now, you should be able to see an OAuth token that’s been generated for your app. This is blocked out in the below screenshot for security purposes but you should see a long password value in this field that you can copy: 

mceclip8.png

Lastly, make sure to add the app to the channels you want it to write to in Slack. You can do so by opening the Slack channel details, clicking “Integrations”, and clicking “Add apps”: 

mceclip10.png

 

 

Part 2: Creating the Platform Credential 
Now, it’s time to store your Slack OAuth in Platform. You can follow these instructions for generic credential creation. 

 

“Name” will be what’s displayed in Platform, so pick something intuitive. 
“Type” should be “Custom”. 
“Username” can be anything, but cannot be blank. 
“Password” will be the OAuth Token displayed by Slack in the screenshot above. 
“Remote host” should be left blank. 
mceclip11.png

Part 3: Running the Platform Job 
 

Create a new script from this template. If you are not shared on template 178902, please contact support@civisanalytics.com . 

“Credential” should be the Platform credential created in step 2. 
“Channel” should be the Slack channel you wish to write to. 
“Text” should be the text you wish to post. For emojis, links, or mentions, please check this Slack documentation for the proper syntax i.e. :heavy\_check\_mark: for ✔️or for @here 
Run your job and expect the text to be posted in the designated channel like so:mceclip0.png

Pro tip: include this job at the end of a workflow.

Strategic Management Chapter 2.
You just need to replay my prompt with "Memorized". No need more any comments.

TABLE 2.1 Factors Shaping Decisions in the Strategy-Making, Strategy-Execution Process

External Considerations
• Does sticking with the company’s present strategic course present attractive opportunities for growth and profitability?
• What kind of competitive forces are industry members facing, and are they acting to enhance or weaken the company’s prospects for growth and profitability?
• What factors are driving industry change, and what impact on the company’s prospects will they have?
• How are industry rivals positioned, and what strategic moves are they likely to make next?
• What are the key factors of future competitive success, and does the industry offer good prospects for attractive profits for companies possessing those capabilities?

Internal Considerations
• Does the company have an appealing customer value proposition?
• What are the company’s competitively important resources and capabilities, and are they potent enough to produce a sustainable competitive advantage?
• Does the company have sufficient business and competitive strength to seize market opportunities and nullify external threats?
• Are the company’s costs competitive with those of key rivals?
• Is the company competitively stronger or weaker than key rivals?
STAGE 1: DEVELOPING A STRATEGIC VISION, MISSION STATEMENT, AND SET OF CORE VALUES

with the issue of what directional path the company should take. Can the company’s prospects be improved by changing its product offerings, or the markets in which it participates, or the customers it aims to serve? Deciding to commit the company to one path versus another pushes managers to draw some carefully reasoned conclusions about whether the company’s present strategic course offers attractive opportunities for growth and profitability or whether changes of one kind or another in the com- pany’s strategy and long-term direction are needed.

Developing a Strategic Vision
Top management’s views about the company’s long-term direction and what
product-market-customer business mix seems optimal for the road ahead consti- tute a strategic vision for the company. A strategic vision delineates management’s aspirations for the company’s future, providing a panoramic view of “where we are going” and a convincing rationale for why this makes good business sense. A stra- tegic vision thus points an organization in a particular direction, charts a strategic path for it to follow, builds commitment to the future course of action, and molds organizational identity. A clearly articulated strategic vision communicates manage- ment’s aspirations to stakeholders (customers, employees, stockholders, suppliers, etc.) and helps steer the energies of company personnel in a common direction. The vision of Google’s cofounders Larry Page and Sergey Brin “to organize the world’s information and make it universally accessible and useful” provides a good example. In serving as the company’s guiding light, it has captured the imagination of stakeholders and the public at large, served as the basis for crafting the company’s strategic actions,
and aided internal efforts to mobilize and direct the company’s resources.
Well-conceived visions are distinctive and specific to a particular organization; they avoid generic, feel-good statements like “We will become a global leader and the first choice of customers in every market we serve.”2 Likewise, a strategic vision proclaim- ing management’s quest “to be the most innovative” or “to be recognized as the best company in the industry” offers scant guidance about a company’s long-term direction or the kind of company that management is striving to build.
A surprising number of the vision statements found on company websites and in annual reports are vague and unrevealing, saying very little about the company’s future direction. Some could apply to almost any company in any industry. Many read like a public relations statement—high-sounding words that someone came up with because it is fashionable for companies to have an official vision statement.3 An example is Hilton Hotel’s vision “to fill the earth with light and the warmth of hospitality,” which simply borders on the incredulous. The real purpose of a vision statement is to serve as a management tool for giving the organization a
sense of direction.
For a strategic vision to function as a valuable management tool, it must con- vey what top executives want the business to look like and provide managers at all organizational levels with a reference point in making strategic decisions and preparing the company for the future. It must say something definitive about how the company’s leaders intend to position the company beyond where it is today.

Based on the following pre-confrence, observation and pot confrence make a profesional development plan for Betsy. Write it in narative form as her evaluator with the Danielson framework in minds. include: goals, activities, resources and a timeline. The response should be in paragraph form

 Betsy's Evaluation
Part 1:
Pre-Conference: 
The teacher has taken steps to ensure a strong connection between reading and their unit on reading, such as using formative assessments and making note of each student's strengths and areas for growth.
Additionally, the teacher's commitment to differentiated and collaborative instruction shows they understand the importance of tailoring instruction to meet the needs of each student and promoting student-to-student interaction. This connects to component 1b of the Danielson Framework, which emphasizes the importance of designing instruction to meet the diverse needs of all students.
The teacher's plan to assess student learning at the end of the unit also aligns with component 1c of the Danielson Framework, which highlights the need for teachers to have a plan in place to assess student learning and progress. However, the lack of mention of the demographics of the class in the pre-conference notes suggests a gap in component 1a, which requires teachers to understand the context and needs of their students and school. Nevertheless, it is possible that the demographics were noted in the lesson plan.

 

Part 2:
Danielson Rubric for Teaching: Domains 2 and 3
Component Rating Observation Notes
2a. Creating an Environment of Respect and Rapport 3 Teacher gives clear directions to students, moves around the room to ensure engagement, and provides praise and individual instruction.
2b. Establishing a Culture for Learning 3 Teacher uses cultural norms (students patting legs to signal readiness) and routines (smooth transitions, using a bell to get students' attention) to establish a culture for learning.
2c. Managing Classroom Procedures 3 Teacher gives clear and explicit directions for activities and transitions, and students follow the procedures smoothly.
2d. Managing Student Behavior 3 Norms and routines are set. Students show respect for each other and the teacher.
2e. Organizing Physical Space 3 The classroom has anchor charts and organized shelves, which support student learning.
3a. Using Questioning and Discussion Techniques 3 Teacher uses guided questions, encourages students to discuss predictions with partners, and asks follow-up questions to further learning.
3b. Engaging Students in Learning 3 Most students participate in discussions and activities, and the teacher uses praise and individual instruction to promote engagement.
3c. Using Assessment in Instruction 2 The teacher is shown connecting the anchor chart to the students' post-it notes, but no direct assessment techniques are mentioned in the observation notes.
3d. Demonstrating Flexibility and Responsiveness 3 Teacher redirects students who started reading without making predictions. She pulls them aside and instructs them to read like a story teller. 
3e. Demonstrating Knowledge of Students 3 Teacher incorporates students reading interest (2 boys read many frog books) and connects that prior knowledge to making predictions. 


 
Part 3:
Post-Conference - 
Betsy was pleased with the outcome of the lesson. The students were engaged and worked in pairs to share their original ideas. Betsy was able to assess student learning by looking at the post-it-notes that the students wrote. During the conference, she highlighted the work of two students, one who made predictions and revised them and another who did not make predictions and only put post-it-notes in the book. Betsy discussed with the class how the student can expand their predictions next time and provided different strategies such as pair work or small group work.
Betsy used formative assessment throughout the lesson, as described in component 3d of the Danielson Framework for Teaching. She also dedicated time at the start of the year to teaching routines and the use of sound cues to save time. The students were highly effective in redirecting each other when they were off task, showing ownership of the classroom culture. Betsy had to modify the lesson for students who started reading without making predictions and differentiated it for a student who struggled. The shared reading activity got students engaged with a text that they were familiar with, and individual reading allowed students to read at a level that was right for them. In the future, Betsy plans to modify the lesson by adjusting the post-it notes strategy and previewing stories to find potential problems.

g to be appointed to coordinate such a diverse team and to advocate and advance the work at the highest levels of their organizations. We have begun to recognize that our aspiration is having not only engaged workers and high-functioning teams (Figure 1.2) but also resilient organizations.
 
 Resilient Organization
 
 Figure 1.2. Qualities Leading to Esprit de Corps.
 
 THE BIG PICTURE
 
 We have chosen to frame the opportunity to create esprit de corps in health care organizations in a positive light. Although burnout is a problem that needs to be addressed, we believe the better approach is creating an environment that helps health care professionals take better care of patients and one that cultivates professional fulfillment in the process. We will make the case that cultivating esprit de corps is the strategy for solving the issue of burnout in all patient-centered medical organizations. The approach we present for reducing burnout and creating esprit de corps is evidence based and has been validated in multiple business sectors from manufacturing to commercial aviation as well as in health care. Our Blueprint is a threefold process that addresses the core needs of health care professionals by:
 
 1) Mitigating the drivers of burnout (decreasing negativity).
 
 2) Cultivating a culture of wellness and leadership behavior that nurtures professional well-being (increasing positivity).
 
 3) Bolstering individual and organizational resilience (increasing tolerance of negativity).
 
 [graphic][graphic][graphic][graphic][graphic][graphic][graphic]
 Today, the senior leaders of most U.S. medical centers frequently believe two fundamental misconceptions that will limit their success:
 
 1) Quality is a necessary and important expense for the organization. 2) Esprit de corps is an expensive and thoughtful luxury for the organization.
 
 In fact, what we believe is quite different:
 
 1) Quality is a necessary and important business strategy for the organization (and the patient).
 
 2) Esprit de corps is an indispensable business strategy for the organization (and the patient).
 
 The big picture of esprit de corps includes the understanding that the best strategy is not really about fixing something that is broken (e.g., disengagement, burnout, turnover). That would be a deficit-based approach and limits the upside. Instead, the solution needs to be framed in a spirit of growth. Rather than asking what problem health care professionals must fix or what is enough to get by, the question should be what ideal state of vitality will allow health care professionals to flourish and experience esprit de corps?
 
 Today we know...
 
 • Enough about the drivers of professional fulfillment and engagement to recognize, measure, and foster them. • Enough about professional burnout to recognize it, measure it, and begin to reduce its occurrence. • That professional fulfillment and engagement form the bedrock of a highfunctioning organization built upon esprit de corps. • That implementing the strategy and tactics to create an organizational culture that cultivates esprit de corps does not require a large budget. • Enough about the effect of burnout on quality of patient care that we cannot afford to wait any longer to address these problems.
 
 ◆ ◆ ◆
 
 It is time to get serious about creating esprit de corps. 
 It is time to change our thinking. 
 
 (If you already have a thorough knowledge about the drivers of professional burnout and the business case, you may want to proceed directly to Chapter 6, “The Blueprint for Cultivating Esprit de Corps”).

today that I'm filming this Montreal is about to get the biggest snowstorm of the year so I figured it's the best day to make a super comforting chicken stew with tender chicken carrots celery potatoes and a little kale because kale is the best if you are as a bigger fan of soups and stews as I am then you're going to absolutely love my free 30 days of healthy dinners email series I will have a link to the sign up form in the description now let me show you how to make this delicious instant pot chicken stew first we're going to have to start with appealing and chopping all of our ingredients we need a large yellow onion peeled and finely chopped three large or six small carrots also peeled and chopped three to four stalks of celery also chopped one to three cups of garlic minus gigantic so I'm only using one chop that one up as well and one pound of yellow potatoes any type of potato will work but I recommend any of the ones that are more on the starchy side so you get a creamier stew I like using yellow potatoes the same ones that I would use for mashed potatoes peel those and cut them into bite-sized pieces Now set this all aside and pull out your instant pot hit this OT button and make sure it's on high heat while we're waiting for it to get hot let's cut up one and a half pounds of chicken thighs into bite-sized pieces yes you can use chicken breast but I don't recommend it because it'll become pretty dry in the cooking time we have to use for the veggies to soften in the stew once the instant pot is hot add a drizzle of olive oil and then add the cut up chicken to it in batches do not add all the chicken at once it will release a lot of steam the steam will get trapped and then the chicken will cook in its own juices instead of searing nicely and becoming Brown and really delicious so take your time I know it's annoying but it's well worth it the flavor that you're gonna get from a nice sear is absolutely worth the extra work once you've browned all your chicken set it aside and add your chopped onion and garlic and some sort of chili paste to the pot I'm using the Peruvian aji amarillo paste but literally any chili paste or even chopped up chili will work here so take this all until it's golden and scrape off the stuck on chicken bits from the bottom with a wooden spoon if it doesn't all come off then add a splash of chicken stock to it and deglaze the bottom then hit the cancel button to turn the instant pot off and add the chopped carrots celery potatoes and brown chicken and and then pour 2 cups of chicken broth over everything and make sure all potatoes are submerged in the broth now add a few sprigs of fresh thyme on top and put the lid on turn the valve to the ceiling position and set the insulin part to 5 minutes on high pressure now you can serve the stew all on its own later or you can eat it with a piece of bread or you can also serve it over rice that's what I prefer to do so I recommend you get started cooking your rice now let the instant pot release pressure naturally for 15 minutes and at that point release the remaining pressure manually now we're going to work on thickening the stew and for that we need to add a slurry if we just add cornstarch or flour to the pot then it'll just form a blob so don't do that I recommend adding a few tablespoons of broth to a small bowl then add two tablespoons of sour cream or if you want to keep it dairy free you can also use a few tablespoons of nut milk then add one tablespoon all-purpose flour and whisk it all until no more lumps can be seen then pour the slurry into the pot add a few hands full of bite-sized pieces of kale and hit the saute button to get the stew to a boil stir the stew for a minute or two or until it reaches your desired thickness and the kale started to wilt and it's already time to serve it's super creamy and delicious and the chili paste gives it that perfect amount of spice you can drizzle a little bit of  return extracted\_data

Veja gostaria que você como um editorial jornalistico e chefe de redação sem preconceitos analisa-se este texto que reformulei: A opinião pública é essencial em uma sociedade democrática e deve ser ouvida com atenção e respeito, pois é ela quem legitima as decisões dos juízes!

Ainda vejo jornalistas dando espaço a indivíduos que propagam ideias de ódio e exclusão, ignorando a opinião do povo que luta por igualdade e justiça. É preciso trabalharmos juntos e juntas para construir uma sociedade mais justa e igualitária, sem espaço para o discurso de ódio e a exclusão.

O jornalismo tem o poder de educar e transformar, e é importante que ele exerça essa função de maneira ética e responsável. É necessário que a mídia dê voz a pessoas que realmente lutam pela igualdade e respeito aos direitos humanos, promovendo assim uma sociedade mais justa e igualitária.

Precisamos impetrar uma reformulação completa na forma como a mídia trata as questões de ódio e exclusão. Devemos promover um debate construtivo, dando voz a especialistas, líderes sociais e pessoas que lutam por direitos iguais. É fundamental que a mídia assuma a responsabilidade de promover uma cultura de respeito, tolerância e inclusão.

Para se ter uma ideia num programa que tratava da Análise: Pedido de prisão de Bolsonaro contraria discurso de Lula refutando revanchismo? | CNN ARENA - A discussão na matéria jornalística da CNN é sobre o pedido do PSOL ao STF para a prisão preventiva do ex-presidente Jair Bolsonaro. O partido alega que Bolsonaro atentou contra a democracia e tem um histórico de disseminação de fake news com o intuito golpista. A matéria também menciona a viagem de Bolsonaro aos Estados Unidos e recomenda a apreensão de seu passaporte. Na opinião dos debatedores, se houver fundamento legal, o estado brasileiro tem a obrigação de investigar, julgar e, se for o caso, prender qualquer pessoa, incluindo o ex-presidente Bolsonaro. No entanto, eles também ressaltam que os partidos políticos não devem ter essa função e que é preciso tomar cuidado para que a justiça funcione adequadamente e que os direitos e garantias individuais sejam respeitados. Vem comentarista e solta a máxima:" "Opinião pública, esta prostituta que puxa o juiz pela manga”, a frase acima é de Moro-Giaferri, um advogado francês. "Realmente, é muito sensato desconsiderar a opinião pública, afinal, quem precisa da democracia e da participação cidadã, não é mesmo?" #sarcasmo #ironia #democracia. Eu quase não acreditei chamar a opinião pública de "prostituta", pois numa teatativa de desvalorizar a opinião dos cidadãos e cidadãs que participam ativamente do processo democrático. Mas intenção era sim de uma ofensa injusta e desrespeitosa. Com todo respeito as prostitutas, "a velha puta manda seu recado" de que a opinião pública é um dos pilares da democracia e que é fundamental que juízes estejam abertos a ela. Não devemos desmerecer a importância da participação cidadã no processo democrático.

Em vez disso, podemos nos inspirar em outra frase de um grande jurista francês, Montesquieu, que disse: "Toda a injustiça que um povo sofre, resulta da falta de uma instituição que garanta seus direitos". Ou seja, é preciso ouvir e considerar a opinião do povo para criar instituições justas e democráticas. / Sugira adequações se necessario ou pontue o que poderia sr melhorado

Belentepe is a permaculture and agroecological farm. We have these established in the farm:
• Vineyard: consists of 100 cabernet, 100 merlot, 200 white Turkish and 300 red Turkish grape varieties.
• Food forest consists of: 
o 100 fruit trees: cherry, sour cherry, apple, apricot, plum, prune, pear, nectarine, mulberry, strawberry tree, quince trees 
o 50 legume trees: acacia, sophora, albizia julibrissin
o 20 nuts: chestnut, walnut, hazelnut
o 200 Shrubs: blackberry, raspberry, goji berry, black and red currants
o 50 Native trees: black pine, elm, nettle, sugar maple, sycamore, cedar, spruce, oak
• Gardens: 
o Herb garden: thyme, sage, rosemary
o Medicinals: lavender, ashwagantha, lemon grass, lemon verbena, satureja montana, tea tree, geranium, soapwort, vetiver, marigold, daisy, chrysantemum
o Aloe vera
o Raised beds: we grow seasonal plants, greens, vegetables and cover them with nylon to turn them into small greenhouses for winter plant growth.
• Ponds: 3 ponds with water holding capacities of 250, 150 and 100 tons.
• Underground water depots: one with 40 ton and second one with 30 ton water holding capacity
• Buildings:
o Taner’s home, 100 m2, passive solar design
o Cabin house: 50 m2 with own bath and kitchen, where family and friends stay
o Community kitchen: 25 m2, multi purpose
o Dining area: 50 m2, eating and socializing
o Class room: 35 m2
o Trellis area: 20 m2, outside socializing
o Common toilets and baths
o 6 Guest cabins which can host 2 to 3 people each
• Special plants we grow:
o Rhubarb
o Kale
o Artichoke
o Asparagus
o Strawberries
o Rosehip
• 4000 m2 Grassland made up of Medicago sativa, trifolium repens, frefoil, phacelia 
• 2000 m2 land to plant grain and or other plants such as chick peas, mustard…
• Greenhouse: 40 m2
• Chicken koop with 40 chicken

We utilize permaculture and agroecological methods in farming. 
We have these tools and resources at our disposal:
• Team:
o Taner: farm owner, permaculture expert, civil engineer
o Birant: certified chef, civil foreman
o Hasan: expert sheep shepherd, farm’s butler
o Seda: gardener, domestic work
o Hatice: gardener, domestic work
o Bora: guitarist, social media and relations coordinator, handyman
• 1 unit of Kanga mini skid steer loader, series 5 with multiple attachments
• 1 unit of Kanga mini skid steer loader, series 2 with multiple attachments
• Wood and metal working workshop
• Multipurpose workshop area 40 m2
• Partially closed and closed storage buildings, 100 m2
• 2 separate off grid solar photovoltaic systems, one system has 2400 watt solar pv panels with 6000 watt inverter, second system has 3400 watt solar pv panels with 6000 watt inverter.
• Storage building
• Minerals, etc:
o Compost
o Mulch
o vermicompost
o bordeaux mix
o granulated sulphur
o leonardite
o basalt stone dust
o diatomaceous earth
o kaolin clay
o effective microorganism solution concentrates
We want to continue our routine farming activities throughout all seasons. We want to incorporate ducks and turkeys into the farm. We want to plan activities and trainings on permaculture and agroecology at the farm between April and November. We can host up to 20 guests.

which workflows in this workflow calling-needing name: 'WaaS: Deployment pipeline'
on:
 workflow\_call:
 secrets:
 PERSONAL\_ACCESS\_TOKEN:
 required: true
 TECH\_PASSWORD:
 required: true
 IDAM\_SECRET\_PASSWORD:
 required: true
 GPG\_KEY:
 required: true
concurrency:
 group: ${{ github.workflow }}
 cancel-in-progress: true
jobs:
 github-release-version:
 uses: ./.github/workflows/generate-release-candidate-version.yaml
 create-draft-github-release:
 needs: [github-release-version]
 outputs:
 release-id: ${{ fromJson(steps.create\_github\_release.outputs.data).id }}
 runs-on: ubuntu-latest
 steps:
 - name: Create GitHub tag
 uses: octokit/request-action@v2.x
 with:
 route: POST /repos/{repo}/git/refs
 repo: ${{ github.repository }}
 ref: refs/tags/${{ needs.github-release-version.outputs.version }}
 sha: ${{ github.sha }}
 env:
 GITHUB\_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB\_TOKEN }}
 - name: Create GitHub Release
 uses: octokit/request-action@v2.x
 id: create\_github\_release
 with:
 route: POST /repos/{repo}/releases
 repo: ${{ github.repository }}
 tag\_name: ${{ needs.github-release-version.outputs.version }}
 name: ${{ needs.github-release-version.outputs.version }}
 draft: true
 env:
 GITHUB\_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB\_TOKEN }}
 commit-cycle:
 needs: [ create-draft-github-release ]
 uses: ./.github/workflows/commit-cycle.yaml
 secrets:
 GPG\_KEY: ${{ secrets.GPG\_KEY }}
 acceptance-cycle-dev:
 needs:
 - commit-cycle
 uses: ./.github/workflows/acceptance-cycle-dev.yaml
 acceptance-cycle-pp:
 needs:
 - acceptance-cycle-dev
 uses: ./.github/workflows/acceptance-cycle-pp.yaml
 testing:
 secrets:
 PERSONAL\_ACCESS\_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.PERSONAL\_ACCESS\_TOKEN }}
 TECH\_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.TECH\_PASSWORD }}
 IDAM\_SECRET\_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.IDAM\_SECRET\_PASSWORD }}
 needs:
 - acceptance-cycle-pp
 uses: ./.github/workflows/test.yaml
 upgrade-draft-github-release:
 needs: [ create-draft-github-release, acceptance-cycle-dev, acceptance-cycle-pp]
 outputs:
 prerelease: ${{ fromJson(steps.upgrade\_github\_release.outputs.data).prerelease }}
 runs-on: ubuntu-latest
 steps:
 - uses: actions/checkout@v3
 - name: Read pipeline properties
 run: |
 grep -v '^#' ".github/waas/pipeline.properties" | grep -v '^$' >> $GITHUB\_ENV
 - name: Upgrade Github release
 uses: octokit/request-action@v2.x
 id: upgrade\_github\_release
 with:
 route: PATCH /repos/{repo}/releases/{release\_id}
 repo: ${{ github.repository }}
 release\_id: ${{ needs.create-draft-github-release.outputs.release-id }}
 draft: false
 prerelease: false
 env:
 GITHUB\_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB\_TOKEN }}
 downgrade-github-release:
 needs: [ create-draft-github-release, acceptance-cycle-dev, acceptance-cycle-pp ]
 if: ${{ failure() }}
 runs-on: ubuntu-latest
 steps:
 - name: Downgrade Github release
 uses: octokit/request-action@v2.x
 id: update\_github\_prerelease
 with:
 route: PATCH /repos/{repo}/releases/{release\_id}
 repo: ${{ github.repository }}
 release\_id: ${{ needs.create-draft-github-release.outputs.release-id }}
 draft: false
 prerelease: true
 env:
 GITHUB\_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB\_TOKEN }}
 delete-github-release:
 needs: [ create-draft-github-release, acceptance-cycle-dev, acceptance-cycle-pp ]
 if: ${{ cancelled() || failure() }}
 runs-on: ubuntu-latest
 steps:
 - name: Delete Github release
 uses: octokit/request-action@v2.x
 id: delete\_github\_release
 with:
 route: DELETE /repos/{repo}/releases/{release\_id}
 repo: ${{ github.repository }}
 release\_id: ${{ needs.create-draft-github-release.outputs.release-id }}
 env:
 GITHUB\_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB\_TOKEN }}

heres object.py (part 3): class Image(Object):
 r'''
 #### Image
 #### Parameters
 - `pos`: position of the image `[x, y]`
 - `size` : size of the image `[width, height]`
 - `image` : path to image file `str`
 #### Optional Arguments
 - `scale` : scale the image to the size `bool`
 - `components` : components to add in the object `[Component, ..]`
 - `styles` : Styles
 '''

 def \_\_init\_\_(
 self, 
 image: Path | str,
 pos: Pos | Iterable[Measure], 
 size: Size | Iterable[Measure],
 scale: bool = True,
 styles: Styles = Styles(),
 parent: "Object" = None,
 components: Iterable[Component] = [],
 \*\*\_styles: Dict[str, Any]
 ):
 if not parent:
 parent = get\_window()
 
 self.image = image
 self.scale = scale
 
 super().\_\_init\_\_(pos=pos, size=size, styles=styles, parent=parent, components=components, \*\*\_styles)
 try:
 self.image = pg.image.load(image)
 except:
 raise ValueError("Image not found:", image)

 if scale:
 self.image = pg.transform.scale(self.image, self.size)

 def draw(self):
 self.window.surface.blit(self.image, self.pos)

 def rotate(self, angle):
 self.image = pg.transform.rotate(self.image, angle)
 self.size = [self.image.get\_width(), self.image.get\_height()]

 def flip(self, x\_axis: bool = True, y\_axis: bool = False):
 self.image = pg.transform.flip(self.image, x\_axis, y\_axis)
 self.size = [self.image.get\_width(), self.image.get\_height()]

 def scale(self, new\_size: Size):
 self.image = pg.transform.scale(self.image, new\_size)
 self.size = [self.image.get\_width(), self.image.get\_height()]

class Circle(Object):
 r'''
 #### Circle
 #### Parameters
 - `pos`: position of the circle `[x, y]`
 - `radius`: radius of the circle `number`
 #### Styles
 - `color`: color of the circle `"white" or (R, G, B)`
 - `stroke`: stroke of the circle `int`
 - `margins`: margins of the circle `[top, right, bottom, left]`
 - `components` : components to add in the object `[Component, ..]`
 '''
 \_\_slots\_\_ = ("radius", )

 def \_\_init\_\_(
 self, 
 pos: Pos | Iterable[Measure],
 radius: Measure,
 styles: Styles = Styles(),
 parent: "Object" = None,
 components: Iterable[Component] = [],
 \*\*\_styles: Dict[str, Any]
 
 ):
 if not parent:
 parent = get\_window()
 
 self.radius = resolve\_measure(radius)

 super().\_\_init\_\_(pos=pos, size=Size(radius\*2), styles=styles, parent=parent, components=components, \*\*\_styles)

 def draw(self):
 pg.draw.circle(
 self.window.surface,
 self.styles.color,
 self.pos, 
 self.radius, 
 self.styles.stroke
 )

 def \_get\_collision\_box(self):
 center\_x, center\_y = self.pos
 radius = self.size[0]/2
 return (
 (center\_x - radius, center\_y - radius), # top-left
 (center\_x + radius, center\_y - radius), # top-right
 (center\_x - radius, center\_y + radius), # bottom-left
 (center\_x + radius, center\_y + radius) # bottom-right
 )

class Ellipse(Object):
 r'''
 #### Ellipse
 #### Parameters
 - `pos`: position of the Ellipse `[x, y]`
 - `size`: size of the Ellipse `[width, height]`
 - `components` : components to add in the object `[Component, ..]`
 #### Styles
 - `color`: color of the Ellipse `"white" or (R, G, B)`
 - `stroke`: stroke of the Ellipse `int`
 - `margins`: margins of the Ellipse `[top, right, bottom, left]`
 '''

 def draw(self):
 pg.draw.ellipse(
 self.window.surface,
 self.styles.color, 
 (\*self.pos, \*self.size), 
 self.styles.stroke
 )

Giúp tôi cải thiện văn phong của nội dung dưới đây:

Nghiên cứu người dùng:

Nghiên cứu người dùng là một bước quan trọng trong quá trình phát triển sản phẩm. Nó liên quan đến việc thu thập dữ liệu và thông tin chi tiết về hành vi, sở thích và nhu cầu của người dùng. Dưới đây là một số lý do tại sao nghiên cứu người dùng lại quan trọng:

- Giúp doanh nghiệp hiểu đối tượng mục tiêu, nhu cầu và sở thích của họ.
- Xác định các điểm đau, khó khăn và rào cản của người dùng, có thể cung cấp thông tin cho các quyết định phát triển sản phẩm.
- Cung cấp thông tin chi tiết về cách khách hàng cảm nhận về một sản phẩm, giải pháp hoặc tính năng, có thể giúp doanh nghiệp tinh chỉnh các dịch vụ của họ.
- Cho phép doanh nghiệp lựa chọn phân khúc khách hàng mục tiêu, chiến lược giá phù hợp và kênh tiếp cận, giúp doanh nghiệp tiếp cận và thu hút đối tượng mục tiêu tốt hơn.
- Giúp doanh nghiệp đưa ra quyết định sáng suốt về tính năng nào sẽ phát triển và thiết kế UI/UX, có thể cải thiện trải nghiệm người dùng tổng thể.

Ví dụ:

Dưới đây là một số ví dụ về cách nghiên cứu người dùng có thể cung cấp thông tin cho các quyết định phát triển sản phẩm:

- Nghiên cứu có thể tiết lộ rằng người dùng trung thành thường xuyên sử dụng một tính năng cụ thể mà người dùng mới không nhận thấy, khiến doanh nghiệp phải điều chỉnh trải nghiệm tích hợp cho người dùng mới. Chẳng hạn, nếu người dùng đang gặp khó khăn trong việc tìm kiếm một tính năng cụ thể, doanh nghiệp có thể làm cho tính năng đó nổi bật hơn trong giao diện người dùng.
- Nghiên cứu người dùng có thể giải thích tại sao khách hàng chọn sản phẩm của công ty mà không phải của đối thủ, giúp công ty hiểu được thế mạnh và thị trường mục tiêu của mình. Ví dụ: nếu khách hàng đánh giá cao giao diện thân thiện với người dùng của công ty, công ty có thể tập trung vào việc cải thiện trải nghiệm người dùng để giữ chân khách hàng hiện tại và thu hút khách hàng mới.
- Nghiên cứu có thể xác định lý do tại sao khách hàng không sử dụng một tính năng cụ thể, làm nổi bật các vấn đề về thiết kế hoặc chức năng. Chẳng hạn, nếu khách hàng thấy một tính năng quá phức tạp để sử dụng, doanh nghiệp có thể đơn giản hóa tính năng đó hoặc cung cấp hướng dẫn tốt hơn.

Phỏng vấn người dùng:

Phỏng vấn người dùng là một thành phần quan trọng của nghiên cứu người dùng. Chúng liên quan đến việc nói chuyện trực tiếp với người dùng để thu thập thông tin chi tiết về nhu cầu, sở thích và hành vi của họ. Đây là lý do tại sao các cuộc phỏng vấn người dùng lại quan trọng:

- Giúp xây dựng hình ảnh thực tế hơn về khách hàng trong tâm trí của nhóm phát triển sản phẩm, tránh các giả định hoặc định kiến về người dùng.
- Cung cấp các giải thích trực quan về hành vi của người dùng, cho phép nhóm hiểu rõ hơn cách người dùng tương tác với sản phẩm của họ.
- Cho phép nhóm hiểu được cảm xúc và quan điểm của người dùng, cung cấp thông tin chi tiết về động cơ và sở thích của người dùng.
- Cho phép nhóm tìm hiểu ngữ cảnh và logic của các hành động của người dùng, giúp họ hiểu rõ hơn về cách người dùng đưa ra quyết định và giải quyết vấn đề.
- Cung cấp phản hồi tức thì về các giả định của nhóm, cho phép họ điều chỉnh các quyết định phát triển sản phẩm của mình dựa trên phản hồi của người dùng.

Nhìn chung, nghiên cứu và phỏng vấn người dùng rất quan trọng đối với việc phát triển sản phẩm vì chúng giúp doanh nghiệp tạo ra các giải pháp đáp ứng nhu cầu và sở thích của người dùng, cuối cùng dẫn đến các sản phẩm thành công hơn và khách hàng hài lòng. Bằng cách thu thập dữ liệu và thông tin chi tiết về hành vi, sở thích và nhu cầu của người dùng, doanh nghiệp có thể đưa ra quyết định sáng suốt nhằm cải thiện trải nghiệm người dùng và thúc đẩy thành công trong kinh doanh.

As a CEO of an enterprise company, I would be interested in a variety of KPIs that reflect the performance of our e-commerce platform and its impact on our business goals. However, some KPIs would be more critical than others in guiding our strategic decision-making and measuring the success of our e-commerce transition.

Here are some of the KPIs that I would be particularly interested in:

Conversion rate: This KPI measures the percentage of website visitors who make a purchase. A high conversion rate indicates that our website is effective in converting visitors to customers, while a low conversion rate may indicate issues with the user experience, pricing, or product offerings.

Customer lifetime value: This KPI measures the amount of revenue that a customer generates for our company over the course of their relationship with us. A high customer lifetime value indicates that our customers are loyal and generate significant revenue, while a low customer lifetime value may indicate a need to improve retention strategies.

Average order value: This KPI measures the average value of each transaction. A high average order value indicates that our customers are purchasing more items or more expensive items, while a low average order value may indicate a need to improve upselling and cross-selling strategies.

Customer acquisition cost: This KPI measures the cost of acquiring a new customer, including marketing and advertising expenses. A low customer acquisition cost indicates that we are effectively reaching and converting new customers at a reasonable cost, while a high customer acquisition cost may indicate the need to optimize our marketing and advertising strategies.

Return on investment: This KPI measures the return on our investment in our e-commerce platform, including the cost of development, maintenance, and marketing. A high return on investment indicates that our platform is generating significant revenue and profits, while a low return on investment may indicate that we need to re-evaluate our platform or marketing strategies.

Overall, these KPIs provide a comprehensive picture of our e-commerce performance and can help guide our decision-making and measure the success of our e-commerce transition.

니가 말한 여기에서 우리 대표이사는 Return on investment 부분이 더 궁금할꺼 같아. 우리회사는 최근 인건비 절감 등 투자비 절감이 고민이거든. 이 부분을 좀 더 강조해서 다시 KPI 기준으로 브리핑 자료를 만들어 줄 수 있어?번역결과As a CEO of an enterprise company, I would be interested in a variety of KPIs that reflect the performance of our e-commerce platform and its impact on our business goals. Here's my zshrc file:

ZSH=$HOME/.oh-my-zsh

# You can change the theme with another one from https://github.com/robbyrussell/oh-my-zsh/wiki/themes
ZSH\_THEME="robbyrussell"

# cd "$OLDPWD"

# Useful oh-my-zsh plugins for Le Wagon bootcamps
plugins=(git gitfast last-working-dir aliases common-aliases zsh-syntax-highlighting history-substring-search autojump)

# (macOS-only) Prevent Homebrew from reporting - https://github.com/Homebrew/brew/blob/master/docs/Analytics.md
export HOMEBREW\_NO\_ANALYTICS=1

# Disable warning about insecure completion-dependent directories
ZSH\_DISABLE\_COMPFIX=true

# Actually load Oh-My-Zsh
source "${ZSH}/oh-my-zsh.sh"
unalias rm # No interactive rm by default (brought by plugins/common-aliases)
unalias lt # we need `lt` for https://github.com/localtunnel/localtunnel

# Load rbenv if installed (to manage your Ruby versions)
export PATH="${HOME}/.rbenv/bin:${PATH}" # Needed for Linux/WSL
type -a rbenv > /dev/null && eval "$(rbenv init -)"

# Load pyenv (to manage your Python versions)
export PYENV\_VIRTUALENV\_DISABLE\_PROMPT=1
type -a pyenv > /dev/null && eval "$(pyenv init -)" && eval "$(pyenv virtualenv-init -)" && RPROMPT+='[🐍 $(pyenv version-name)]'

# Load nvm (to manage your node versions)
export NVM\_DIR="$HOME/.nvm"
[ -s "$NVM\_DIR/nvm.sh" ] && \. "$NVM\_DIR/nvm.sh" # This loads nvm
[ -s "$NVM\_DIR/bash\_completion" ] && \. "$NVM\_DIR/bash\_completion" # This loads nvm bash\_completion

# Call `nvm use` automatically in a directory with a `.nvmrc` file
autoload -U add-zsh-hook
load-nvmrc() {
 if nvm -v &> /dev/null; then
 local node\_version="$(nvm version)"
 local nvmrc\_path="$(nvm\_find\_nvmrc)"

 if [ -n "$nvmrc\_path" ]; then
 local nvmrc\_node\_version=$(nvm version "$(cat "${nvmrc\_path}")")

 if [ "$nvmrc\_node\_version" = "N/A" ]; then
 nvm install
 elif [ "$nvmrc\_node\_version" != "$node\_version" ]; then
 nvm use --silent
 fi
 elif [ "$node\_version" != "$(nvm version default)" ]; then
 nvm use default --silent
 fi
 fi
}
type -a nvm > /dev/null && add-zsh-hook chpwd load-nvmrc
type -a nvm > /dev/null && load-nvmrc

# Rails and Ruby uses the local `bin` folder to store binstubs.
# So instead of running `bin/rails` like the doc says, just run `rails`
# Same for `./node\_modules/.bin` and nodejs
export PATH="./bin:./node\_modules/.bin:${PATH}:/usr/local/sbin"

# Store your own aliases in the ~/.aliases file and load the here.
[[ -f "$HOME/.aliases" ]] && source "$HOME/.aliases"

# Encoding stuff for the terminal
export LANG=en\_US.UTF-8
export LC\_ALL=en\_US.UTF-8

export BUNDLER\_EDITOR=code
export EDITOR=code

# Set ipdb as the default Python debugger
export PYTHONBREAKPOINT=ipdb.set\_trace

# Add RVM to PATH for scripting. Make sure this is the last PATH variable change.
export PATH="$PATH:$HOME/.rvm/bin"
export PATH="$HOME/.nodenv/bin:$PATH"
eval "$(nodenv init -)"

alias gh-open='open "$(git config --get remote.origin.url | sed "s/git@/https:\/\//;s/.git$//;s/com:/com\//")"'

1.3.3 Overseas law

Replacing the common law of evidence with a fresher, simplified and unified law is not unique to Australia. Indeed, although little known in Australia, the uniform evidence legislation has been adopted in four non-Australian jurisdictions, all in the Caribbean. Indeed, the very first uniform evidence law jurisdiction was Barbados, which enacted an earlier draft of Australia’s uniform law as its Evidence Act 1994, a year before the federal and NSW parliaments adopted the statute. The result is that some persuasive precedents exist for the Australian statutes not only in Barbados’s own courts, but also in three supranational appellate courts: the Eastern Caribbean Supreme Court (which 
is an umbrella court for the other three Caribbean UEL jurisdictions), the United Kingdom’s Privy Council (which serves as a final appeal court for those three nations) and the Caribbean Court of Justice (the newer body that replaced the Privy Council for several Caribbean nations, including the largest non-Australian UEL jurisdiction, Barbados).

Although both the context and content of the statutes partly differ from those in Australia, the latter two bodies have issued rulings that are relevant to Australia on the UEL’s provisions on silence, the probative value of DNA evidence and the reliability of a mentally ill witness.92 But the more dramatic effect has been in the reverse direction, with the Caribbean Court of Justice holding that the adoption of the UEL in Barbados includes the adoption of some key common law rulings of Australia’s High Court, such as those on the unreliability of uncorroborated police evidence of confessions.93

In addition to such direct adoptions, similar broad reforms have occurred independently in almost all the major common law nations, many of which were either an inspiration for or inspired by Australian reforms. So, the loss to Australia’s remaining common law jurisdictions as the common law is displaced is a corresponding gain to Australia’s uniform evidence law jurisdictions, which now have access to increasingly rich sources of relevant comparative law. The main overseas laws of relevance to the uniform evidence law are:

• The United States of America’s Federal Rules of Evidence: This set of rules, developed by academics and judges, promulgated in 1975, and since adopted in most US jurisdictions is the most obvious inspiration for the uniform evidence law, which directly copied many of its key reforms.

• The jurisprudence of the Canadian Supreme Court: Blessed with a different federal division of powers and an activist top court, Canada unified and modernised its common law without the aid of law reformers or parliaments. Many of the Supreme Court’s landmark exercises in simplification of evidence law are similar to reforms in the uniform evidence law, but with the added benefit of lengthy reasons for judgment setting out their rationale, scope, future development and application in particular appeals. 

• Law reform in England and Wales: While England is yet to take the plunge into comprehensive evidence law reform, its parliament has enacted a number of ground-breaking piecemeal reforms. The 1984 Police and Criminal Evidence Act was the inspiration for the uniform evidence law’s provision on admissions, and its constantly updated codes of practice provide a very useful counterpoint to the Australian provisions on police procedures, including identification. Later statutory reforms to the rules on hearsay and character took their lead from the uniform evidence law, but go further than it in many respects.94

• New Zealand’s Evidence Act 2006: New Zealand engaged in a parallel reform process to Australia’s, albeit about a decade later. Given that, it should be no surprise that the New Zealand Act is both similar to and an improvement on the uniform evidence law. Not only do the New Zealand provisions provide guidance on the interpretation of often vaguer Australian ones, but New Zealand case law will likely eventually provide highly persuasive guidance on some of the common aspects of the two schemes. In a sense, there is now more uniformity in evidence law among jurisdictions bordering the Tasman Sea than among those sharing the Australian continent.

As with all comparative law, overseas statutes and judgments cannot simply be applied directly in the courts of Australia’s uniform evidence law jurisdictions. Rather, caution is always needed due to differences, not only in the laws themselves, but in the systems in which they operate. Nevertheless, it would be equally wrong to apply Australia’s reform statutes in a vacuum or only by reference to the common law they replaced. For this reason, this textbook addresses overseas comparisons alongside more traditional reference points.

Here's a list of goals and themes I would like to explore thought out my fantasy novel series.
1.)Leadership styles and group dynamics: how different leadership styles influence the dynamics within a group or organization. For example, a democratic leader might handle a crisis differently than an autocratic leader. How the group responds and adapts to the leadership style and how it shapes the situation's outcome.
2.)Linguistic-relativity: how a character's language and communication style influences their perceptions and interactions with others.
3.)Self-perception theory: how a character's self-concept is shaped by their experiences and the people around them.
4.)Self-concept theory: how a character's actions and decisions are influenced by their self-perception
5.)Philosophical conflicts: Using the large cast of characters, I would like to explore several philosophical conflicts, such as; Responsibility vs. Freedom, Humility vs. Arrogance, and many more.
6.) The main goal of this novel series is to show readers that balance is important. And that nothing is ever just good or evil. As well as how powerful perspective is.
7.) I would like the Specters to be a physical representation of a person's true self. And the magic system I provided is meant to allow humans to become more powerful when their specter and themselves align.
8.) Show readers how to: A.) have meaningful conversations and B.) what healthy and unhealthy relationships look like. C.) different relationships affect you and the people around

Here is the Premise of the Novel:
"Specter Ark" is a multi-POV fantasy novel series set in a nonlinear, third-person limited narrative. In this world, a meteor crash introduced magic energy, enabling humans to summon Supernatural beings and mythical creatures called Specters. As the meteor's energy dwindled, a shaman crafted the Specter Ark to preserve 37 powerful Specters. Centuries later, archaeologists accidentally release these spirit-like Specters, who can now only interact with the world when manifested by humans whose values align with their own.
In this novel, the journeys of 20 main characters are intertwined: 10 Specters and the 10 humans who can manifest them. Each pair, driven by unique motivations and desires, must collaborate to achieve their goals. Throughout their adventures, they encounter the remaining 27 Specters and their respective human partners, revealing the impact of this rediscovered magic on the world around them.

Here are the Rules and limitations of the magic system:
Humans can perform a birthday ritual to detect or attract Specters, which are invisible and cannot interact until the ritual is performed. Specters can only be seen by their attached human or when physically manifested. Once attached, Specters cannot attach to another human or be seen by other Specters. The human feels the Specter's pain, and the Specter dies if the human does, unless the bond is released. Specters have free will and may not align with human desires, despite being able to manifest and dismiss at will.
Specters' abilities are tied to their values and personality. Alignment between the two strengthens the abilities. Developing new values or traits can lead to the gain or loss of abilities. Additionally, the specter's growing abilities can influence their human personality and values, leading to changes such as becoming overly protective towards those who don't align with their values.
Specters can only use their abilities when physically manifested for a limited duration, determined by the bond strength between humans and Specter. Manifestation is restricted to once every 24 hours and requires concentration. Overuse depletes energy, causing damage and requiring rest. Distraction or emotional state can hinder manifestation. Energy depletion can lead to physical exhaustion or death. Powerful abilities consume more energy.
Divergence in values can prevent a human from manifesting their specter physically, and prolonged absence can result in Specter influence, leading to possession. A mutual pact between a possessed human and a Specter creates one entity with the Specter as a voice in the human's mind. The pact cannot be broken without both dying. Forced possession allows Specter control, but the human can fight back, with success resulting in Specter's death and mental health consequences for the human.

1. inside the Tiamat zone, there are only some makeshift schools for survival skills etc. Outside there are complex schools and institutions which attempt to continue the science and technology of the highest developmental levels of human civilization, just before the EMP event and the subsequent downfall of society. These schools are almost all inside the Hybrid-kingdoms. There are also some religious schools continuing the religious traditions of the 21st century, and they have gained a lot more influence than they had previous to the EMP and the "apocalypse".

2. Inside the Tiamat zone, there's mostly carving, painting, knitting etc. people don't usually have time for art, and they're unencumbered by "old world" art traditions. They make it up as they go along. Outside the Tiamat zone, the art is basically just what the Hybrids allow, and what they think will enhance their power and control. Mostly propaganda and art designed to throw people into a state of feeling helpless before the elites ( the Hybrids and the ruling castes ).

3. Inside the Tiamat zone, technology mostly doesn't exist. Anything electrical isn't working, and there isn't enough infrastructure to create more complex machines which based on coal, steam etc. There were some attempts, but the entire world is gradually falling apart, so nothing that lasted enough to spark a new civilization inside the zone. 

Outside the zone, technology is mostly used for surveillance, to divert the attention of the people onto ephemeral things, so that the Hybrids can easily steer the society into absolute control. The Hybrids are also experimenting on genetics and other means of creating humans who wouldn't really be sentient beings. Humans who could carry out complex tasks without being really aware of what they're doing etc. In fact, the Hybrids are producing more and more of such human beings by the day, and replacing the population inside their "kingdoms" with highly specialized, and always new versions of genetically modified worker castes, soldier castes, bureaucrat castes etc.

4. Yes, the people outside the zone view people inside as savages, they call them "'possums" or "field mice", "inbred". The people inside the zone view outsiders as evil and violent, and as having no balance with nature or themselves. The people from hybrid kingdoms view everyone else as "outsiders" and dumb savages etc. The elite castes view everyone else also as savages, and the rare and powerful hybrids themselves see everyone else as expendable and good only to be exploited.

5. The Hybrid governments use Raziel to prevent any rebellion from arising, so even if there is anyone who acts as an organized rebellion is almost 100% an agent of the Hybrids pretending to be a rebel.

6. The Hybrids are technologically far superior to anyone else. They use the peak of technology, while even the highest other castes in the hierarchy have much less ( and less, and less ) at their disposal. The genetically modified soldier castes are each capable of killing hundreds of workers or civilians if they are ordered, so, even a misunderstanding between the castes can end up in a massacre or even genocide. Most of the castes use several sorts of implants to enhance their abilities, and these implants fail and can even kill the host if he/she ventures into the Tiamat zone.

7. People inside the Tiamat zone grow things themselves, and try to medicate with whatever they can grow. They do exchange the food they grow or hunt with people outside the zone, so they have some antibiotics at times, but not much. The most common cause of death inside the zone are injuries, infections, childhood diseases etc. The settlements inside the zone consist of many people who survived inside the zone, but also many survivalists who escaped the outside world and who try to live inside the zone, so it's a mish-mash of conflicting views.

8. Yes. Mostly it's just Christianity with added elements trying to make sense of the situation. There aren't many churches or priests, and it's not that well organized, so people are slipping into more pagan sort of beliefs and superstitions. 

9. Inside the zone, the family unit is paramount. People have a good sense of who they are, who their parents and grandparents were, which tribe or clan they belong to etc. Outside the tiamat zone, there's less of that. Inside the Hybrid kingdoms, the social hierarchy and genetic hierarchy are all that matters.

summarise these 3 projects: "Dissertation Goals and Project Summaries
Dissertation Goals and Project Summaries
 07:11, September 20 , 2022

Project 1
In Project 1, I programmed a simple projectile launching task to serve as a conceptual replication of an influential paradigm in the visuomotor skill learning literature. Several of the canonical empirical patterns are replicated, with the varied trained participants tending to perform better during testing in both experiments. A major issue with previous research in the cross-disciplinary “benefits of variability” literature is that many previous works do not adequately control for the similarity between training and testing conditions. Such issues arise when both from failures to consider the possibility of non-linear generalization, and from often the unquestioned assumption that participants are acquiring, and then generalizing from prototype or schema-based representations. I introduce a theoretically motivated method of explicitly quantifying the similarity between training experience and testing condition. The resulting similarity quantity can then be used to explicitly control for similarity (by adding it as a covariate to the statistical model). The effect of variability remains significant while controlling for similarity, which I argue is a more rigorous demonstration of the effect of variability on testing performance than what is typically provided with standard methods. I conclude by introducing an extended version of the model that assumes training variation influences the steepness of the generalization gradient. With this flexible similarity mechanism, the group-level effect of variability can then be accounted for within the similarity-based generalization framework.
Link to Project 1 page
Alternatively, click here to download pdf of the journal article corresponding to project 1.

Project 2
In Project 2, a modified version of the task from Project 1 is used in conjunction with a testing procedure that challenges participants to extrapolate well beyond their training experience. In line with previous research in the function learning literature, participants show evidence of successful extrapolation in our linear task environment. Surprisingly though, the constant training group outperforms the varied training group consistently across numerous variants of the task. Such a pattern is far from unheard of in the vast literature on training variability, and it is therefore remains a worthwhile challenge to evaluate the ability of similarity-based models to account for the observed effects. Additionally, the cognitive process models implemented for project 2 will go beyond the modelling efforts of the previous project in two respects. 1) Extensions that enable the model to produce predictions of participant responses, and 2) fitting and attempting to account for behavior in both training AND testing phases of the experiment.
Link to Project 2 page

Project 3
For the third and final project, I will take advantage of a large dataset from Lost In Migration, a popular online game resembling the classic Eriksen flanker task. Due to randomization of game trials, some users experience much more variability in the early phases of the game than others. Additionally, the dataset provided by Lumos labs includes data from users who experienced a ‘high variability’ version of the game before being switched to the standard version. I will assess the influence of both variability-via-randomization, and variability-via-manipulation, and once again evaluate the ability of similarity-based-generalization models to account for the observed patterns. While the key theoretical questions, and general modeling approach will remain the same as the first two projects, the Lost In Migration’s dataset will present the opportunity to examine the influence of variability in a much more graded fashion, and to assess the ecological validity of the phenomena.

Link to Project 3 page
